metadata.title: Flora and Fauna of Indian States
metadata.description: Comprehensive guide to biodiversity across India's states and union territories
metadata.biodiversity_rank: India is one of the 17 megadiverse countries, hosting 7-8% of all recorded species

state_animal.name: Blackbuck
state_animal.funfact: Blackbucks can leap up to 2 meters high and run at speeds of 80 km/h - faster than most predators! Males have beautiful spiraling horns that can grow up to 70cm long.

state_bird.name: Indian Roller (Palapitta)
state_bird.funfact: The Indian Roller performs spectacular aerial acrobatics during mating season, rolling and tumbling through the air - hence its name! Its feathers display 8 different shades of blue.

state_tree.name: Neem
state_tree.funfact: Neem is called the 'Village Pharmacy' in India because every part - leaves, bark, seeds, flowers - has medicinal properties. It can purify air and soil, and one neem tree produces enough oxygen for 10 people!

state_flower.name: Water Lily
state_flower.funfact: Water lily flowers open in the morning and close at night, following the sun! The ancient Egyptians considered it sacred, and it can survive in water as deep as 2.5 meters.

notable_wildlife.name: Olive Ridley Sea Turtle
notable_wildlife.funfact: Thousands of female Olive Ridley turtles arrive simultaneously at Andhra's beaches for 'arribada' (mass nesting) - one of nature's most spectacular events! They return to the same beach where they were born decades ago.

notable_wildlife.name: Fishing Cat
notable_wildlife.funfact: Unlike most cats, fishing cats love water! They have partially webbed paws and can dive underwater to catch fish. They tap the water surface to mimic insect movements and lure fish.

notable_wildlife.name: Indian Pangolin
notable_wildlife.funfact: Pangolins are the world's only scaly mammals and the most trafficked animal globally. When threatened, they curl into a perfect ball that even lions can't unroll! One pangolin can eat 70 million ants per year.

endemic_species.name: Jerdon's Courser
endemic_species.funfact: This bird was thought to be extinct for 86 years until it was rediscovered in 1986 in Andhra Pradesh! It's a nocturnal bird that's so rare, fewer than 50 individuals may exist.

national_parks.name: Sri Venkateswara National Park
national_parks.key_species: Sloth Bear, Indian Giant Squirrel, Golden Gecko
national_parks.funfact: This park is home to the rare Golden Gecko, a lizard that glows golden in sunlight and can detach its tail to escape predators!

state_animal.name: Gayal (Mithun)
state_animal.funfact: The Mithun is a semi-domesticated bovine used as currency in tribal societies! It's considered more valuable than money - bride prices and fines are calculated in Mithuns. They can climb steep mountain slopes with ease.

state_bird.name: Great Hornbill
state_bird.funfact: Female hornbills seal themselves inside tree cavities during nesting, leaving only a small hole. The male feeds her through this hole for 4 months! Their casque (helmet) is so light it's hollow inside despite looking solid.

state_tree.name: Hollong
state_tree.funfact: Hollong trees can grow up to 50 meters tall - as high as a 15-story building! Their seeds have wing-like structures that spiral down like helicopters when falling.

state_flower.name: Foxtail Orchid (Lady's Slipper)
state_flower.funfact: This orchid grows on tree branches without harming the tree (epiphyte). Its flowers smell like grape candy and bloom in cascading bunches of 100+ flowers that last for weeks!

notable_wildlife.name: Red Panda
notable_wildlife.funfact: Red pandas are not related to giant pandas - they're more closely related to raccoons! They have a 'false thumb' (extended wrist bone) to grip bamboo, and they spend 13 hours a day eating because bamboo has so little nutrition.

notable_wildlife.name: Clouded Leopard
notable_wildlife.funfact: Clouded leopards have the longest canine teeth relative to body size of any cat - up to 2 inches! They can hang upside down from branches using their hind legs and rotate their ankles 180 degrees.

notable_wildlife.name: Takin
notable_wildlife.funfact: Takins are called 'goat-antelopes' and look like a mix between a cow and a goat. According to legend, the Takin was created when a Tibetan saint attached the head of a goat to the body of a cow!

notable_wildlife.name: Mishmi Takin
notable_wildlife.funfact: This subspecies secretes an oily substance on its coat that acts as a natural raincoat! They can survive in temperatures as low as -20°C.

notable_wildlife.name: Snow Leopard
notable_wildlife.funfact: Snow leopards can't roar (unlike other big cats) but they can purr! Their tail is almost as long as their body and so thick they use it as a scarf to wrap around their face while sleeping in freezing temperatures.

endemic_species.name: Bugun Liocichla
endemic_species.funfact: Discovered only in 2006, this bird is so rare that fewer than 20 individuals have ever been spotted! It's one of the newest bird species discovered in India in the last 50 years.

endemic_species.name: Arunachal Macaque
endemic_species.funfact: This macaque was discovered in 2004 and can survive at altitudes up to 3,500 meters - higher than any other macaque! It's the only primate discovered in India in modern times.

national_parks.name: Namdapha National Park
national_parks.key_species: Snow Leopard, Red Panda, Clouded Leopard, White-winged Wood Duck
national_parks.funfact: Namdapha is the only park in the world that has four big cat species - Tiger, Leopard, Snow Leopard, and Clouded Leopard! It ranges from 200m to 4,500m altitude.

unique_flora.name: Blue Vanda Orchid
unique_flora.funfact: This stunning blue orchid is so rare and valuable that it's called the 'Queen of Orchids'. A single plant can sell for thousands of dollars! Its true blue color is extremely rare in nature.

state_animal.name: Indian One-horned Rhinoceros
state_animal.funfact: The Indian rhino has 'armor-plated' skin with folds that look like riveted metal plates! Despite weighing 2,000+ kg, they can run at 55 km/h and are excellent swimmers. Their horn is made of keratin (like your fingernails), not bone.

state_bird.name: White-winged Duck
state_bird.funfact: Also called 'White-winged Wood Duck', this is one of the world's rarest ducks with fewer than 1,000 individuals left! They nest in tree holes 10-15 meters high, and ducklings jump down to the ground within 24 hours of hatching.

state_tree.name: Hollong
state_tree.funfact: The same as Arunachal Pradesh's state tree. These giants can live for over 500 years! The wood is so durable it was used to build traditional Assamese houses that last centuries.

state_flower.name: Foxtail Orchid
state_flower.funfact: Assam has over 500 species of orchids! The Foxtail Orchid's flowers hang in dense clusters like a fox's tail and are used in traditional Assamese medicine to treat skin diseases.

notable_wildlife.name: Asiatic Wild Water Buffalo
notable_wildlife.funfact: Wild water buffaloes are the ancestors of domestic buffaloes and are the largest bovine species! Bulls can weigh up to 1,200 kg and their horns can span 2 meters. They once killed a tiger in Kaziranga!

notable_wildlife.name: Hoolock Gibbon
notable_wildlife.funfact: India's only ape species! Hoolock gibbons are monogamous and 'sing' elaborate duets with their partners every morning to defend territory. They can swing through trees at 55 km/h using just their arms (brachiation).

notable_wildlife.name: Pygmy Hog
notable_wildlife.funfact: The world's smallest and rarest wild pig, standing only 25cm tall! Once thought extinct, they were rediscovered in 1971. They build elaborate nests for protection and are the only pig species that does so.

notable_wildlife.name: Bengal Florican
notable_wildlife.funfact: During mating season, male Bengal Floricans perform spectacular aerial displays, jumping 2 meters high while flapping wings! Fewer than 1,000 individuals remain globally.

notable_wildlife.name: Ganges River Dolphin
notable_wildlife.funfact: These dolphins are effectively blind! They navigate using echolocation in muddy river waters. They swim on their side and can only survive in freshwater. India declared it the National Aquatic Animal in 2009.

endemic_species.name: Assam Roofed Turtle
endemic_species.funfact: Once thought extinct, this turtle was rediscovered in 2002! It has a distinctive roof-like shell. Conservationists are now breeding them to save the species from extinction.

national_parks.name: Kaziranga National Park
national_parks.key_species: One-horned Rhino, Tiger, Elephant, Wild Water Buffalo
national_parks.funfact: Kaziranga has the world's largest population of one-horned rhinos (over 2,400)! It's also one of the few places where you can see a tiger swimming. Two-thirds of the park floods during monsoon, forcing animals to migrate to higher ground.

national_parks.name: Manas National Park
national_parks.key_species: Pygmy Hog, Golden Langur, Hispid Hare, Tiger
national_parks.funfact: Manas is a UNESCO World Heritage Site and the only tiger reserve in India with the rare Golden Langur! It's named after the serpent goddess Manas and borders Bhutan's Royal Manas National Park.

famous_tea_biodiversity.fact: Assam tea gardens are biodiversity hotspots hosting over 400 bird species!
famous_tea_biodiversity.unique_species: Tea gardens provide habitat for elephants, leopards, and the rare Assam Macaque that loves to steal tea leaves.

state_animal.name: Gaur (Indian Bison)
state_animal.funfact: The Gaur is the world's largest bovine, with bulls weighing up to 1,500 kg! Despite their massive size, they can jump over 2-meter fences. Tigers are their only natural predator, and even tigers often fail to bring down a healthy adult.

state_bird.name: House Sparrow
state_bird.habitat: Urban and rural areas near humans
state_bird.funfact: Sparrows are disappearing from Indian cities at an alarming rate - down 80% in some areas! They've lived alongside humans for 10,000 years. March 20 is celebrated as World Sparrow Day to raise awareness.

state_tree.name: Peepal (Sacred Fig)
state_tree.funfact: Buddha attained enlightenment under a Peepal tree 2,500 years ago! These trees can live for over 3,000 years and grow on buildings (strangler fig). They release oxygen even at night, unlike most trees.

state_flower.name: Marigold (Genda)
state_flower.funfact: Marigolds are offered in every temple in Bihar and used in all festivals! The flowers are edible and used in medicines. One marigold plant can produce 50+ flowers in a season.

notable_wildlife.name: Gharial (Fish-eating Crocodile)
notable_wildlife.funfact: Gharials have the narrowest snout of any crocodilian - perfect for catching fish! Males develop a bulbous growth on their snout called a 'ghara' (pot) used to make buzzing sounds to attract females. They can't walk on land like other crocodiles.

notable_wildlife.name: Indian Wolf
notable_wildlife.funfact: Indian wolves are smaller than their northern cousins and live in packs of 3-5. They're genetically distinct and may be a separate species! The famous 'Jungle Book' wolves were likely this species.

notable_wildlife.name: Smooth-coated Otter
notable_wildlife.funfact: These otters hunt in groups and can take down 4-foot catfish! They're incredibly playful and create water slides on riverbanks just for fun. They use 12 different vocalizations to communicate.

national_parks.name: Valmiki National Park (Valmiki Tiger Reserve)
national_parks.key_species: Tiger, Leopard, Sloth Bear, Gharial
national_parks.funfact: Named after sage Valmiki who wrote the Ramayana. It's part of the larger Terai Arc landscape shared with Nepal. Wild elephants migrate between India and Nepal through this park.

river_ecosystem.name: Gangetic Dolphins in Bihar
river_ecosystem.funfact: This is Asia's only sanctuary exclusively for river dolphins! The 60 km stretch of Ganges here hosts the highest concentration of Gangetic dolphins in the world.

state_animal.name: Wild Water Buffalo (Van Bhainsa)
state_animal.funfact: Wild water buffaloes in Chhattisgarh's Indravati National Park are among the last pure genetic populations! They're excellent swimmers and can stay submerged for several minutes. A single kick can kill a tiger.

state_bird.name: Hill Myna
state_bird.funfact: Hill Mynas are better mimics than parrots! They can perfectly copy human speech, other bird calls, and even mechanical sounds like car alarms. In captivity, they can learn 100+ words and phrases.

state_tree.name: Sal (Sakhua)
state_tree.funfact: Sal trees can live for 150+ years and grow to 50 meters! Their wood is termite-resistant and was used to build railway sleepers across India. Lord Buddha was born, attained enlightenment, and died under Sal trees.

state_flower.name: Not officially designated
state_flower.note: Chhattisgarh doesn't have an officially declared state flower yet
state_flower.common_flowers: Marigold, Jasmine, various orchids in forest regions

notable_wildlife.name: Bastar Hill Myna
notable_wildlife.funfact: Bastar region's hill mynas are so sought after for their talking ability that they're threatened by illegal wildlife trade. Tribals traditionally keep them as pets and teach them local dialects!

notable_wildlife.name: Flying Squirrel
notable_wildlife.funfact: Flying squirrels don't actually fly - they glide up to 150 meters between trees using a furry membrane! They're nocturnal and can make 90-degree turns in mid-air. Their large eyes glow red in flashlight.

notable_wildlife.name: Indian Giant Squirrel (Shekru)
notable_wildlife.funfact: These squirrels can grow up to 3 feet long (including tail) and have spectacular multi-colored fur - maroon, black, cream, and brown! They can leap 20 feet between trees and eat while hanging upside down.

notable_wildlife.name: Sloth Bear
notable_wildlife.funfact: Sloth bears inspired Baloo in 'The Jungle Book'! They can close their nostrils completely and use their lips like a vacuum cleaner to suck up termites. They carry their cubs on their back - the only bear species to do so.

national_parks.name: Indravati National Park
national_parks.key_species: Wild Water Buffalo, Tiger, Leopard, Barasingha
national_parks.funfact: Named after the Indravati River, this park has one of the last populations of genetically pure wild water buffaloes. It's also home to the rare Hill Myna that can mimic 40+ different sounds!

national_parks.name: Kanger Valley National Park (Kanger Ghati)
national_parks.key_species: Tiger, Leopard, Mouse Deer, Flying Squirrel
national_parks.funfact: Famous for the spectacular Tirathgarh Waterfalls and mysterious Kotumsar Caves - one of the longest natural caves in India at 330 meters! The caves host blind fish that evolved in total darkness.

tribal_biodiversity_connection.fact: Chhattisgarh has 42 tribal communities with deep forest connections
tribal_biodiversity_connection.traditional_knowledge: Tribals use over 500 medicinal plants and have sustainable harvesting practices that have protected forests for millennia
tribal_biodiversity_connection.funfact: The Gond tribe's artwork depicts hundreds of local animals and plants with incredible accuracy, serving as a biodiversity database!

state_animal.name: Gaur (Indian Bison)
state_animal.funfact: Goa's Gaurs are particularly large because of the lush Western Ghats habitat! Bulls can stand 2.2 meters at the shoulder. They have a distinctive dorsal ridge and dewlap (neck fold) that makes them look imposing.

state_bird.name: Flame-throated Bulbul (Ruby-throated Bulbul)
state_bird.funfact: This bird is found only in the Western Ghats! Males have a brilliant ruby-red throat that glows in sunlight. They're excellent singers and create complex melodious songs to attract mates.

state_tree.name: Matti (Asna/Indian Laurel)
state_tree.funfact: Matti wood is so hard and durable it was used to build Portuguese-era ships! The tree can grow in rocky, shallow soil where other trees can't. Its bark is used to treat dysentery.

state_flower.name: Not officially designated
state_flower.note: Goa doesn't have an officially declared state flower

notable_wildlife.name: Malabar Giant Squirrel
notable_wildlife.funfact: These psychedelic squirrels have purple, orange, black, and maroon fur! They build multiple nests (up to 5) and use different ones for sleeping, eating, and hiding. They're so arboreal they rarely touch the ground.

notable_wildlife.name: King Cobra
notable_wildlife.funfact: The world's longest venomous snake (up to 18 feet)! King Cobras are the only snakes that build nests for their eggs and guard them fiercely. Their venom can kill an elephant, but they're actually quite shy.

notable_wildlife.name: Hump-nosed Pit Viper
notable_wildlife.funfact: This small viper has a distinctive upturned snout! It's responsible for many snakebites in Goa because it's well-camouflaged in leaf litter. Unlike cobras, they give birth to live young (not eggs).

notable_wildlife.name: Indian Grey Hornbill
notable_wildlife.funfact: These hornbills feed on fruits and regurgitate seeds far from the parent tree, making them crucial 'forest farmers'! They have a distinctive bouncing flight pattern like a basketball.

national_parks.name: Mollem National Park
national_parks.key_species: Bengal Tiger, Black Panther, King Cobra, Malabar Giant Squirrel
national_parks.funfact: Part of the Western Ghats UNESCO World Heritage Site! Home to the spectacular Dudhsagar Waterfalls - India's 5th tallest waterfall that looks like flowing milk (Dudhsagar means 'Sea of Milk').

national_parks.name: Bhagwan Mahavir Wildlife Sanctuary
national_parks.key_species: Gaur, Sambar, Leopard, Flying Lizard
national_parks.funfact: Houses the Devil's Canyon - a mysterious gorge with unique rock formations. The sanctuary has over 600 species of flowering plants, many endemic to Western Ghats!

coastal_species.name: Olive Ridley Sea Turtle
coastal_species.funfact: Goa's beaches are crucial nesting sites! Turtle nests are protected by locals and forest department. Baby turtles instinctively rush to sea using the moon's reflection on water.

coastal_species.name: Bottlenose Dolphin
coastal_species.funfact: Dolphin watching is popular in Goa! They're incredibly intelligent, use names to call each other, and sleep with one eye open to watch for predators.

state_animal.name: Asiatic Lion
state_animal.funfact: Gujarat is the ONLY place in the world where Asiatic lions exist in the wild! Once down to just 20 individuals in 1913, conservation efforts have increased the population to 700+. Male Asiatic lions have smaller manes than African lions.

state_bird.name: Greater Flamingo
state_bird.funfact: Gujarat hosts the world's largest breeding colony of flamingos - over 2 lakh birds! Flamingos are pink because they eat shrimp and algae containing carotenoids. They can drink boiling water near geysers and filter salt from water.

state_tree.name: Banyan (Vad)
state_tree.funfact: Banyan trees never die of old age! They keep expanding by growing aerial roots that become new trunks. The largest banyan in India (in Andhra Pradesh) covers 4.7 acres and can shelter 20,000 people!

state_flower.name: Marigold (Galgota)
state_flower.funfact: Marigolds are used in every Gujarati festival and wedding! The flowers contain natural pest control compounds - planting them near vegetables keeps insects away naturally.

notable_wildlife.name: Indian Wild Ass (Khur)
notable_wildlife.funfact: Found ONLY in Gujarat's Little Rann of Kutch! They can run at 70 km/h and survive in salt deserts where temperatures reach 50°C. The population grew from 362 in 1962 to over 6,000 now - a conservation success!

notable_wildlife.name: Chinkara (Indian Gazelle)
notable_wildlife.funfact: Chinkaras can survive without drinking water - they get all moisture from plants! They can run at 80 km/h and leap 4-5 meters. Their name comes from the sneezing 'chink-chink' sound they make.

notable_wildlife.name: Blackbuck
notable_wildlife.funfact: Velavadar has one of the highest blackbuck densities in the world! Males perform spectacular pronking - jumping 2 meters high with all four legs off the ground. Their spiral horns can grow up to 79 cm long.

notable_wildlife.name: Marsh Crocodile (Mugger)
notable_wildlife.funfact: Mugger crocodiles in Gujarat's Vishwamitri River live in urban areas! They're often found in parks and even cross roads in Vadodara city. They can survive in both freshwater and saltwater.

notable_wildlife.name: Great Indian Bustard
notable_wildlife.funfact: One of the world's heaviest flying birds at 15 kg! Fewer than 150 individuals remain. Males perform elaborate courtship displays, inflating their neck sac and making booming calls audible from 500 meters.

national_parks.name: Gir National Park
national_parks.key_species: Asiatic Lion, Leopard, Marsh Crocodile
national_parks.funfact: The Nawab of Junagadh refused to hunt the last 20 lions in 1913, saving the species from extinction! Gir's lions have adapted to climb trees - unusual behavior rarely seen in African lions.

national_parks.name: Marine National Park (Jamnagar)
national_parks.key_species: Coral reefs, Sea turtles, Dolphins, Dugongs
national_parks.funfact: India's first Marine National Park! At low tide, you can walk on exposed coral islands. It has 42 islands and hosts coral species, sponges, and is one of the last habitats of the rare Dugong (sea cow).

national_parks.name: Wild Ass Sanctuary (Little Rann of Kutch)
national_parks.key_species: Indian Wild Ass, Wolf, Caracal, Flamingos
national_parks.funfact: The world's largest compact salt desert! During monsoon, it becomes a shallow wetland attracting millions of flamingos. It's also home to traditional salt farmers who live in temporary settlements.

unique_ecosystems.name: Great Rann of Kutch
unique_ecosystems.funfact: The world's largest salt desert! During dry season, it's a blinding white expanse of salt crust. During monsoon, it transforms into a shallow wetland. It's so flat you can see the Earth's curvature!

state_animal.name: Blackbuck
state_animal.funfact: Haryana's Bhiwani district has significant blackbuck populations that are protected by local Bishnoi communities who consider them sacred! Young blackbucks can accelerate from 0 to 80 km/h in just 3 seconds.

state_bird.name: Black Francolin (Black Partridge)
state_bird.funfact: Males make a distinctive 'chik-chik-chikaara' call at dawn that sounds like they're saying 'I have no fear'! They prefer running to flying and only fly when absolutely necessary. They've adapted well to agricultural landscapes.

state_tree.name: Peepal (Sacred Fig)
state_tree.funfact: Haryana has ancient Peepal trees that are over 1,000 years old! These trees are considered sacred and cutting one is prohibited by law in many states. They host over 20 species of birds and insects.

state_flower.name: Lotus
state_flower.funfact: Lotus is India's national flower too! The flower is self-cleaning - water droplets roll off its petals carrying dirt (lotus effect). Seeds can remain viable for 1,300+ years! Ancient lotus seeds germinated after being dormant for centuries.

notable_wildlife.name: Nilgai (Blue Bull)
notable_wildlife.funfact: Asia's largest antelope! Males are blue-grey (hence 'Nilgai' - blue cow), females are brown. Despite weighing 300 kg, they can leap 2-meter fences. They're protected as 'cows' by many communities.

notable_wildlife.name: Indian Peafowl
notable_wildlife.funfact: India's national bird! A peacock's train has about 200 feathers with eye-spots that appear to stare at predators. Only males have the spectacular tail. They dance before rain and can kill snakes.

notable_wildlife.name: Golden Jackal
notable_wildlife.funfact: Jackals mate for life and the pair stays together for years! They're extremely vocal and their howling chorus at dusk is iconic. They help farmers by eating rodents and crop pests.

notable_wildlife.name: Sarus Crane
notable_wildlife.funfact: The world's tallest flying bird at 1.8 meters! Sarus cranes mate for life and perform elaborate duets - dancing and calling in perfect synchronization. If one dies, the mate often refuses to eat and dies of grief.

wildlife_sanctuaries.name: Sultanpur National Park
wildlife_sanctuaries.funfact: Just 50 km from Delhi, this tiny park hosts Siberian Cranes, flamingos, pelicans, and hundreds of bird species! It's a crucial stopover for birds migrating from Europe to India.

wildlife_sanctuaries.name: Kalesar National Park
wildlife_sanctuaries.funfact: Named after Lord Shiva's temple (Kali+Swar). This park is where the Sal forests of Himalayas meet the scrublands of plains - creating unique biodiversity mixing species from both ecosystems.

conservation_success.fact: Once nearly extinct in Haryana, Chinkaras have made a comeback thanks to community conservation efforts
conservation_success.funfact: Local Bishnoi communities protect them so well that Chinkaras are often seen grazing alongside livestock in villages!

state_animal.name: Snow Leopard
state_animal.funfact: Snow leopards can jump 9 meters (30 feet) in one leap - the distance of 3 cars! Their tail is almost as long as their body and so thick they wrap it around themselves like a blanket in freezing temperatures. They can't roar but make a unique 'chuffing' sound.

state_bird.name: Western Tragopan (Jujurana)
state_bird.funfact: One of the rarest pheasants in the world! Males have a spectacular blue facial skin that they inflate like a balloon during courtship display. They nest in trees (unlike most pheasants) and can fly straight upwards.

state_tree.name: Deodar (Himalayan Cedar)
state_tree.funfact: 'Deodar' means 'Wood of the Gods' in Sanskrit! These majestic trees can live for 1,000+ years and grow to 65 meters tall. Their wood is naturally termite-resistant and was used to build ancient temples. The scent repels insects.

state_flower.name: Pink Rhododendron (Burans)
state_flower.funfact: When rhododendrons bloom, entire mountainsides turn pink! The flowers are edible - locals make juice, jam, and pickles. The petals are rich in Vitamin C. Bees that feed on rhododendron make 'mad honey' that can cause hallucinations!

notable_wildlife.name: Himalayan Brown Bear
notable_wildlife.funfact: Found only in the Himalayas above 3,000m! These bears can weigh up to 300 kg and survive on 70% vegetarian diet. Before hibernating, they eat continuously for weeks and can gain 180 kg!

notable_wildlife.name: Himalayan Tahr
notable_wildlife.funfact: These goat-antelopes have rubber-like hooves that provide excellent grip on near-vertical cliffs! They can climb 60-degree slopes and jump 2 meters to cross ravines. Their thick coat has two layers for extreme cold.

notable_wildlife.name: Musk Deer
notable_wildlife.funfact: Males have vampire-like fangs (tusks) up to 7cm long used for fighting! They produce musk - the world's most expensive animal product ($45,000/kg). They're heavily poached but now protected. They have no antlers.

notable_wildlife.name: Himalayan Monal (State Bird of Uttarakhand too)
notable_wildlife.funfact: The male has 9 different metallic colors that shimmer in sunlight - it's like a living rainbow! They dig for food using their beaks, creating distinctive crescent-shaped marks. They're depicted on the ₹10 coin.

notable_wildlife.name: Himalayan Vulture
notable_wildlife.funfact: One of the highest-flying birds - recorded at 37,000 feet (11,300m)! They can spot a carcass from 4 km away. Their stomach acid is so strong it can digest anthrax and botulinum toxin, preventing disease spread.

national_parks.name: Great Himalayan National Park
national_parks.key_species: Snow Leopard, Musk Deer, Himalayan Tahr, Western Tragopan
national_parks.funfact: A UNESCO World Heritage Site! Has 31 mammal species, 181 bird species, and over 800 plant species. The altitude ranges from 1,500m to 6,000m, creating multiple climate zones in one park!

national_parks.name: Pin Valley National Park
national_parks.key_species: Snow Leopard, Siberian Ibex, Bharal
national_parks.funfact: India's coldest national park! Located in the Trans-Himalayan region, it's a cold desert with temperatures dropping to -25°C. Home to the endangered Himalayan wolf that hunts in packs.

unique_flora.name: Brahma Kamal (Saussurea obvallata)
unique_flora.funfact: Considered the 'King of Himalayan Flowers'! Blooms only once a year at night and is considered sacred. Found above 4,500m altitude. The flower was offered to Lord Shiva according to mythology.

unique_flora.name: Himalayan Blue Poppy
unique_flora.funfact: The stunning blue poppy is Bhutan's national flower and found in HP too! The blue color is so rare that it took botanists decades to verify it wasn't dyed. It grows where few other flowers can survive.

state_animal.name: Indian Elephant
state_animal.funfact: Jharkhand's elephants migrate through ancient corridors they've used for thousands of years! Elephants can detect water sources 5 km away and communicate using infrasound (below human hearing range) across 10 km distances.

state_bird.name: Asian Koel
state_bird.funfact: The Koel is a brood parasite - females lay eggs in crow nests and let crows raise their chicks! Males have a distinctive 'koo-ooo' call that intensifies during monsoon. In mythology, its call announced the arrival of spring.

state_tree.name: Sal (Sakhua)
state_tree.funfact: Jharkhand has some of India's finest Sal forests! Sal leaves are used to make eco-friendly plates (pattal) used in millions across India. The resin is used in incense and traditional medicine. Tribes use Sal leaves for roofing.

state_flower.name: Palash (Flame of the Forest)
state_flower.funfact: When Palash blooms, entire forests turn bright orange-red like they're on fire! The flowers are used in Holi colors, traditional medicine, and to make dye. They're also used to make 'tesu' drink during Holi festival.

notable_wildlife.name: Indian Bison (Gaur)
notable_wildlife.funfact: Saranda means '700 hills' in the local language and has Asia's largest Sal forests! Gaurs here can weigh 1,500 kg. Despite their size, they're shy and run from humans, crashing through the forest like bulldozers.

notable_wildlife.name: Leopard
notable_wildlife.funfact: Jharkhand has one of India's highest leopard densities! Unlike tigers, leopards adapt well near human settlements. They can carry prey twice their weight up a tree and have individual spot patterns like fingerprints.

notable_wildlife.name: Sloth Bear
notable_wildlife.funfact: Jharkhand's sloth bears love to eat honey and mahua flowers! They can close their nostrils and use their lips like a vacuum to suck termites from 5-foot-deep tunnels, creating a sound audible from 100 meters!

notable_wildlife.name: Four-horned Antelope (Chousingha)
notable_wildlife.funfact: The ONLY antelope in the world with four horns! The front pair is tiny (only 2-5 cm). They're so shy that researchers rarely see them. They prefer rocky, hilly terrain and are excellent climbers.

notable_wildlife.name: Dhole (Indian Wild Dog)
notable_wildlife.funfact: Dholes hunt in packs and can take down prey 10 times their size, including tigers! They make whistling sounds to communicate (not barking). Packs share food with sick/injured members - showing remarkable social behavior.

national_parks.name: Betla National Park (Palamau Tiger Reserve)
national_parks.key_species: Tiger, Elephant, Leopard, Gaur
national_parks.funfact: One of India's first tiger reserves! Has the historic Palamau Fort built in 1613 inside the park. During summer, animals gather at waterholes, making it excellent for wildlife viewing.

national_parks.name: Dalma Wildlife Sanctuary
national_parks.key_species: Elephant, Leopard, Barking Deer
national_parks.funfact: Famous for elephant migration! Every year, elephants migrate through this sanctuary using the same routes their ancestors used. Local tribes celebrate the elephant migration as a festival.

tribal_wildlife_connection.fact: Jharkhand has 32 tribal groups with deep forest connections
tribal_wildlife_connection.funfact: Tribals worship Sal trees during Sarhul festival, asking nature's permission before using forest resources. This ancient practice has protected forests for centuries!

state_animal.name: Indian Elephant
state_animal.funfact: Karnataka has India's highest elephant population (over 6,000)! Elephants spend 16 hours daily eating and can consume 150 kg of food. They mourn their dead, covering bodies with leaves and returning to 'graveyards' for years.

state_bird.name: Indian Roller
state_bird.funfact: Called 'Neelkanth' (Blue Throat) after Lord Shiva! During courtship, males perform spectacular aerial acrobatics - diving, rolling, and tumbling. Seeing one on Dussehra is considered extremely auspicious in Karnataka.

state_tree.name: Sandalwood (Chandana)
state_tree.funfact: Karnataka produces 75% of India's sandalwood! The heartwood takes 15-20 years to develop fragrance. It's so valuable (₹15,000/kg) that each tree is GPS-tagged and guarded. It's a parasitic tree that steals nutrients from neighboring tree roots!

state_flower.name: Lotus
state_flower.funfact: The lotus symbolizes purity in Indian culture because it grows in muddy water but remains spotless! Its leaves are superhydrophobic - water forms perfect spheres and rolls off, cleaning the surface (lotus effect used in technology).

notable_wildlife.name: Bengal Tiger
notable_wildlife.funfact: Karnataka's tiger reserves form India's largest contiguous tiger habitat! Tigers have striped skin, not just fur - if shaved, stripes remain. Each tiger's stripe pattern is unique like human fingerprints. They can eat 40 kg in one sitting!

notable_wildlife.name: Indian Leopard
notable_wildlife.funfact: Karnataka has India's highest leopard density! They're incredibly adaptable - living in forests, farms, and even cities. They can survive on just 2 kg of meat per day. Black leopards (black panthers) are common in Karnataka's Kabini region.

notable_wildlife.name: Malabar Giant Squirrel
notable_wildlife.funfact: These rainbow-colored squirrels can grow up to 1 meter long! They build multiple globe-shaped nests (dreys) and use different ones for different purposes. They can jump 6 meters between trees and hang upside down while eating.

notable_wildlife.name: Dhole (Wild Dog)
notable_wildlife.funfact: Karnataka has one of the largest dhole populations in the world! Pack members regurgitate food for sick/injured members. They're so fearsome that even tigers avoid them. A pack of 15 dholes can kill a tiger!

notable_wildlife.name: King Cobra
notable_wildlife.funfact: Agumbe in Karnataka is the 'King Cobra Capital of India'! King cobras are the only snakes that build nests. They can 'stand' up to 6 feet tall (1/3 of their body). Despite deadly venom, they're shy and avoid humans.

notable_wildlife.name: Lion-tailed Macaque
notable_wildlife.funfact: One of the world's rarest monkeys with only 4,000 remaining! They have a magnificent silver-white mane around their face. They're so shy they'll abandon their home range if disturbed. They communicate using 17 different calls.

endemic_species.name: Wroughton's Free-tailed Bat
endemic_species.funfact: This bat exists in only ONE cave in the entire world - Barapede Cave in Karnataka! Discovered in 1913, then 'lost' for 86 years until rediscovered in 2001. Fewer than 200 individuals exist!

endemic_species.name: Malabar Pit Viper
endemic_species.funfact: These beautiful pit vipers come in multiple colors - green, yellow, brown, and even blue! They have heat-sensing pits between their eyes and nostrils to detect warm-blooded prey in complete darkness.

national_parks.name: Bandipur National Park
national_parks.key_species: Tiger, Elephant, Leopard, Gaur, Wild Dog
national_parks.funfact: Part of the Nilgiri Biosphere Reserve - India's first biosphere reserve! Night traffic is banned through the park. It's one of the best places to spot tigers in India with over 150 tigers.

national_parks.name: Nagarhole National Park (Rajiv Gandhi National Park)
national_parks.key_species: Tiger, Elephant, Wild Dog, Leopard
national_parks.funfact: Has India's highest density of herbivores! The Kabini backwaters attract huge herds of elephants - sometimes 200+ together. It's one of the best places to spot black panthers in the world.

national_parks.name: Bannerghatta National Park
national_parks.key_species: Elephant, Leopard, Indian Bison, Sloth Bear
national_parks.funfact: One of the few reserves near a major city (just 22 km from Bengaluru)! Has India's first butterfly park with over 20 species. The park has an elephant corridor connecting Bandipur reserve.

bird_sanctuaries.name: Ranganathittu Bird Sanctuary
bird_sanctuaries.key_species: Painted Stork, Spoonbill, Cormorant, River Tern
bird_sanctuaries.funfact: Karnataka's largest bird sanctuary despite being tiny! Set on 6 islands in Kaveri river. Over 170 bird species nest here. Crocodiles bask on rocks below the bird colonies - an amazing sight!

western_ghats_biodiversity.funfact: The Western Ghats in Karnataka are older than the Himalayas! They're one of the world's 8 'hottest' biodiversity hotspots with more endemic species per sq km than almost anywhere else on Earth.

state_animal.name: Indian Elephant
state_animal.funfact: Kerala's temple elephants are cultural icons! Elephants can recognize themselves in mirrors (only great apes and dolphins can too). They have excellent memories - they remember friends and enemies for decades. A dying elephant will be mourned by the herd for days.

state_bird.name: Great Hornbill
state_bird.funfact: The casque on their head is hollow (despite looking solid) and amplifies their calls! During nesting, females seal themselves in tree hollows with mud - the male feeds her through a small hole for 4 months. They mate for life!

state_tree.name: Coconut Palm
state_tree.uses: Food, oil, coir, timber, thatch - every part is useful
state_tree.funfact: Called 'Kalpavriksha' (wish-fulfilling tree) because every part has a use! A single tree can produce 75-200 coconuts/year for 60-80 years. Coconut water is sterile and was used as emergency IV fluid in WWII!

state_flower.name: Kanikonna (Golden Shower Tree)
state_flower.funfact: When Kanikonna blooms during Vishu (Kerala New Year), entire landscapes turn golden! Seeing Kanikonna first thing on Vishu morning is considered extremely auspicious. The tree is called 'Golden Shower' because flowers fall like golden rain!

notable_wildlife.name: Nilgiri Tahr
notable_wildlife.funfact: Found ONLY in the Western Ghats! These mountain goats have specially adapted hooves that grip slippery rocks. Males have distinctive curved horns and a 'saddle' marking on their back. Only 2,500 remain in the wild.

notable_wildlife.name: Lion-tailed Macaque
notable_wildlife.funfact: Called 'Wanderoo' - one of the world's 25 most endangered primates! Their silver mane makes them look like miniature lions. They spend 95% of their life in trees and rarely come to ground.

notable_wildlife.name: Nilgiri Marten
notable_wildlife.funfact: The only marten species in South India and extremely rare! They're excellent climbers and can rotate their hind legs 180° to climb down trees headfirst. They have a bright yellow-orange throat patch.

notable_wildlife.name: Malabar Civet
notable_wildlife.funfact: One of the world's rarest mammals! Only two confirmed sightings in the last 30 years. They were once common in cashew plantations. Scientists aren't sure if they still exist - they're 'living ghosts'.

notable_wildlife.name: Purple Frog
notable_wildlife.funfact: Discovered only in 2003, this 'pig-nosed frog' looks like a bloated purple balloon! It spends 50 weeks underground and emerges only during monsoon to mate. It's a 'living fossil' - its ancestors split from other frogs 130 million years ago!

notable_wildlife.name: Travancore Tortoise
notable_wildlife.funfact: Found only in the Western Ghats! They can live for over 100 years. During hot weather, they dig burrows and 'aestivate' (summer hibernation). Shell patterns are unique to each individual.

endemic_species.name: Malabar Large-spotted Civet
endemic_species.funfact: Possibly extinct! Last confirmed sighting was in 1987. Scientists have placed camera traps throughout its former range hoping to find survivors. It was once common but vanished mysteriously.

endemic_species.name: Kerala Laughingthrush
endemic_species.funfact: Endemic to the Palani Hills! Their call sounds like maniacal laughter echoing through the misty forests. They live in groups and 'mob' predators by surrounding them while making alarm calls.

national_parks.name: Silent Valley National Park
national_parks.key_species: Lion-tailed Macaque, Nilgiri Langur, Tiger, Leopard
national_parks.funfact: Called 'Silent Valley' because of the absence of cicadas (unlike other Kerala forests)! It has the largest population of lion-tailed macaques. A massive public movement in the 1970s saved it from being dammed!

national_parks.name: Eravikulam National Park
national_parks.key_species: Nilgiri Tahr, Atlas Moth, Nilgiri Langur
national_parks.funfact: Home to the world's largest population of Nilgiri Tahr! During neelakurinji bloom (once every 12 years), the hills turn completely blue-purple with millions of flowers. The 2018 bloom attracted over 1 million visitors!

national_parks.name: Periyar National Park
national_parks.key_species: Elephant, Tiger, Gaur, Lion-tailed Macaque
national_parks.funfact: Built around an artificial lake created by the British in 1895! You can watch elephants bathing and swimming from boats. It's one of the few reserves where you can see wildlife from water. Bamboo rafting safaris are unique here.

backwater_ecosystem.name: Kerala Backwaters
backwater_ecosystem.funfact: The backwaters are a network of 900 km of waterways! They support unique species adapted to both fresh and saltwater. Farmers here practice 'below-sea-level farming' - the only place in India where crops grow below sea level!

unique_flora.name: Neelakurinji
unique_flora.funfact: This flower blooms only once every 12 years! When it does, entire mountainsides turn purple-blue. The Paliyan tribe used to calculate age by counting neelakurinji blooms. Next bloom: 2030!

state_animal.name: Barasingha (Swamp Deer)
state_animal.funfact: 'Barasingha' means '12-tined' referring to the antlers that can have 10-14 points! The hard-ground subspecies found in MP's Kanha is found NOWHERE else in the world. They were down to just 66 individuals in 1970, now recovered to 800+!

state_bird.name: Indian Paradise Flycatcher
state_bird.funfact: Males have magnificent tail feathers up to 30 cm long - longer than their body! Young males are rufous colored but turn pure white as they age. They catch insects mid-air, their long tails streaming behind like ribbons.

state_tree.name: Banyan (Bargad)
state_tree.funfact: A single banyan tree can cover several acres with its aerial roots becoming new trunks! The largest living organism by area is a banyan tree in Andhra Pradesh covering 4.7 acres. MP has ancient banyans over 500 years old.

state_flower.name: White Lily (Madonna Lily)
state_flower.funfact: White lilies symbolize purity and are used in religious ceremonies across MP. A single lily bulb can produce the same flower for decades! They've been cultivated for over 3,000 years - among the oldest cultivated flowers.

notable_wildlife.name: Bengal Tiger
notable_wildlife.funfact: MP is called 'Tiger State of India' with the highest tiger population! Tigers can leap 10 meters horizontally. Each tiger needs about 60 sq km of territory. Kanha inspired Rudyard Kipling's 'The Jungle Book'.

notable_wildlife.name: Indian Leopard
notable_wildlife.funfact: MP has more leopards than any other state! They're incredibly stealthy - a leopard can be 10 feet away and you won't see it. They carry prey 3 times their weight up trees. Each leopard's spots are unique.

notable_wildlife.name: Gaur (Indian Bison)
notable_wildlife.funfact: The world's largest bovine - bulls can weigh 1,500 kg! Despite their size, they can jump 2-meter fences. Even tigers think twice before attacking a healthy adult gaur. They have a distinctive muscular ridge on their back.

notable_wildlife.name: Indian Wild Dog (Dhole)
notable_wildlife.funfact: Dholes are one of the most efficient predators - 90% hunting success rate compared to lions' 30%! They use whistling sounds to coordinate hunts. Pack members share food with nursing mothers and injured individuals.

notable_wildlife.name: Sloth Bear
notable_wildlife.funfact: MP has one of India's highest sloth bear populations! They can vacuum up 10,000 ants in one night. They carry cubs on their back - the only bear species to do this. Their shaggy coat protects against bee stings.

notable_wildlife.name: Indian Wolf
notable_wildlife.funfact: Indian wolves are smaller and more slender than northern wolves. They hunt cooperatively but live in smaller packs of 3-5. They're the wolves featured in Kipling's 'The Jungle Book'!

notable_wildlife.name: Indian Gharial
notable_wildlife.funfact: MP's Chambal Sanctuary is one of the last gharial strongholds! Their narrow snout is perfect for catching fish. Males develop a bulbous growth on their snout called a 'ghara'. Only about 650 adults remain worldwide.

national_parks.name: Kanha National Park
national_parks.key_species: Tiger, Barasingha, Leopard, Wild Dog
national_parks.funfact: Inspired Rudyard Kipling's 'The Jungle Book'! The ONLY place where the hard-ground barasingha exists. It was India's first tiger reserve to have dedicated tourism zones. Has over 300 tigers and 900 barasingha.

national_parks.name: Bandhavgarh National Park
national_parks.key_species: Tiger, Leopard, Sloth Bear, Gaur
national_parks.funfact: Has the highest density of Bengal tigers in India! The 2,000-year-old Bandhavgarh Fort sits atop a cliff in the park. A 35-foot statue of Vishnu carved from rock lies in the forest. Famous tigress 'Sita' was filmed for 15 years!

national_parks.name: Pench National Park
national_parks.key_species: Tiger, Leopard, Wild Dog, Gaur
national_parks.funfact: Another park that inspired 'The Jungle Book'! The Pench River was where Mowgli was found. It spans two states (MP and Maharashtra). Famous tigress 'Collarwali' raised 26 cubs - a record!

national_parks.name: Satpura National Park
national_parks.key_species: Tiger, Leopard, Sloth Bear, Giant Squirrel
national_parks.funfact: The only park in India where walking safaris are allowed inside the core zone! It has prehistoric rock shelters with 10,000-year-old paintings. Elephant safaris take you through dense forests where vehicles can't go.

tiger_conservation.funfact: MP was home to 'Machali' and 'Collarwali' - the two most famous tigresses in the world! Machali was photographed by millions of tourists and even had a documentary on Netflix.

state_animal.name: Indian Giant Squirrel (Shekru)
state_animal.funfact: These magnificent squirrels can grow up to 1 meter long (including tail)! They have spectacular multicolored fur - maroon, orange, cream, and black. They build multiple nests and rarely come to the ground, leaping 6 meters between trees.

state_bird.name: Yellow-footed Green Pigeon (Hariyal)
state_bird.funfact: These beautiful pigeons are so well camouflaged in fig trees that they're almost invisible! Despite being common, they're rarely seen. Their distinctive whistling call is one of the most pleasant bird sounds in Indian forests.

state_tree.name: Mango (Amba)
state_tree.funfact: Maharashtra's Alphonso (Hapus) mango is called the 'King of Mangoes' and exported worldwide! A single tree can produce 400-600 fruits per year for over 100 years. India produces 50% of the world's mangoes.

state_flower.name: Jarul (Pride of India)
state_flower.funfact: When Jarul blooms, the purple-pink flowers are so spectacular it's called 'Pride of India'! The flowers only last one day but new ones bloom continuously. The tree is also called 'Queen's Flower' or 'Giant Crape Myrtle'.

notable_wildlife.name: Bengal Tiger
notable_wildlife.funfact: Tadoba's tigers are famous for being bold and photogenic! Tigers here often walk on safari roads in broad daylight. The tigress 'Maya' of Tadoba was photographed by over a million tourists.

notable_wildlife.name: Indian Leopard
notable_wildlife.funfact: Mumbai's Sanjay Gandhi National Park has the world's highest leopard density in an urban area! About 35 leopards live within the city limits. They've adapted to eat stray dogs and are rarely aggressive to humans.

notable_wildlife.name: Indian Bison (Gaur)
notable_wildlife.funfact: Gaurs in Maharashtra can weigh up to 1,500 kg! They're the largest wild cattle in the world. Despite their intimidating size, they're vegetarian. A charging gaur can derail small vehicles!

notable_wildlife.name: Sloth Bear
notable_wildlife.funfact: Maharashtra's Melghat is one of India's best sloth bear habitats. These bears inspired Baloo in 'The Jungle Book'! They can hang upside down from branches like sloths (hence the name) and eat 10,000 termites in one session.

notable_wildlife.name: Indian Wild Dog (Dhole)
notable_wildlife.funfact: Dholes are so effective as pack hunters that even tigers avoid them! They communicate through whistles and can coordinate attacks using specific calls. In Tadoba, they've been seen chasing tigers away from their kills!

notable_wildlife.name: Forest Owlet
notable_wildlife.funfact: Thought extinct for 113 years until rediscovered in Maharashtra in 1997! Only about 250 individuals exist. Unlike most owls, they're active during the day. They hunt lizards and small birds.

endemic_species.name: Koyna Toad
endemic_species.funfact: Found ONLY in a 10 sq km area near Koyna Dam! Discovered in 2009, it's one of India's most range-restricted amphibians. Climate change threatens to make it extinct within decades.

national_parks.name: Tadoba Andhari Tiger Reserve
national_parks.key_species: Tiger, Leopard, Sloth Bear, Wild Dog, Gaur
national_parks.funfact: Maharashtra's oldest and largest national park! Tigers here are famous for approaching safari vehicles without fear. The legendary tigress 'Maya' was one of the most photographed tigers in the world.

national_parks.name: Sanjay Gandhi National Park
national_parks.key_species: Leopard, Spotted Deer, Wild Boar, Bonnet Macaque
national_parks.funfact: One of the most visited parks in Asia with 2 million visitors annually! It's inside Mumbai city - the only city with a national park inside its limits. Has 35+ leopards living alongside 20 million humans!

national_parks.name: Melghat Tiger Reserve
national_parks.key_species: Tiger, Sloth Bear, Forest Owlet, Gaur
national_parks.funfact: One of the first nine tiger reserves established in India! Has the highest sloth bear density in the country. The rediscovered Forest Owlet (after 113 years) was found here.

notable_species.name: Olive Ridley Sea Turtle
notable_species.funfact: Local villages have transformed from turtle hunters to turtle protectors! The Velas Turtle Festival celebrates conservation and attracts thousands of visitors to watch baby turtles enter the sea.

notable_species.name: Whale Shark
notable_species.funfact: The world's largest fish visits Maharashtra's waters! Local fishermen now release accidentally caught whale sharks and receive compensation. They can grow up to 12 meters long but eat only plankton.

state_animal.name: Sangai (Brow-antlered Deer)
state_animal.funfact: The Sangai is called 'Dancing Deer of Manipur' because of its delicate walking style on floating vegetation! Found ONLY in Manipur's Keibul Lamjao - the world's only floating national park. They walk on phumdis (floating vegetation) that sink under their weight!

state_bird.name: Mrs. Hume's Pheasant (Nongin)
state_bird.funfact: Named after Mary Anne Hume, wife of British naturalist Allan Hume! The male has spectacular steel-blue, maroon, and white plumage with a 90cm tail. They're so shy that even local tribes rarely see them.

state_tree.name: Uningthou (Phoebe hainesiana)
state_tree.funfact: Uningthou is sacred to Manipuri people and represents life and immortality! The tree is never cut and is planted near homes for protection. Ancient Uningthou trees mark historical and sacred sites.

state_flower.name: Siroi Lily
state_flower.funfact: Found ONLY on Siroi Hill in Manipur - nowhere else on Earth! It was discovered in 1946 and named after Mrs. Macklin who first collected it. Local tribes protect it and celebrate the Shirui Lily Festival annually.

notable_wildlife.name: Hoolock Gibbon
notable_wildlife.funfact: India's only ape species! They 'sing' elaborate duets at dawn that can be heard 2 km away. They swing through trees at 55 km/h and can leap 15 meters between branches. They mate for life.

notable_wildlife.name: Slow Loris
notable_wildlife.funfact: The only venomous primate! They have a toxic bite - their saliva mixed with gland secretions causes severe allergic reactions. They move so slowly that algae grows on their fur, helping them camouflage.

notable_wildlife.name: Clouded Leopard
notable_wildlife.funfact: Has the longest canine teeth relative to body size of any living cat! They can climb down trees headfirst and hang upside down from branches. Their cloud-shaped spots provide perfect camouflage.

notable_wildlife.name: Marbled Cat
notable_wildlife.funfact: One of the least-known wild cats! They're incredibly rare and secretive. Their tail is as long as their body and helps them balance on thin branches. They hunt birds and squirrels in trees.

endemic_species.name: Manipur Bush Quail
endemic_species.funfact: Found only in Northeast India! They're so shy they prefer running through grass rather than flying. They live in coveys of 10-20 birds and make soft whistling calls.

national_parks.name: Keibul Lamjao National Park
national_parks.key_species: Sangai, Hog Deer, Otter
national_parks.funfact: The ONLY floating national park in the world! It's located in Loktak Lake - the largest freshwater lake in Northeast India. The phumdis (floating vegetation) are up to 2 meters thick and support trees, wildlife, and even human settlements!

loktak_lake_ecosystem.name: Loktak Lake
loktak_lake_ecosystem.funfact: The only floating biomass ecosystem in the world! Phumdis cover 40% of the lake. Local Keibul people live on floating huts called phumshongs. The lake hosts over 230 species of aquatic plants and 100+ bird species.

state_animal.name: Clouded Leopard
state_animal.funfact: Clouded leopards have the longest canine teeth relative to body size of any cat - up to 2 inches! They can rotate their ankles 180° to climb down trees headfirst. They're so secretive that scientists know very little about their behavior.

state_bird.name: Hill Myna
state_bird.funfact: Hill Mynas can mimic human speech better than parrots! They can learn over 100 words and even mimic emotions and accents. In the wild, they mimic other birds and forest sounds. They're heavily trafficked for the pet trade.

state_tree.name: Gamhar (White Teak)
state_tree.funfact: Gamhar is one of the fastest-growing trees in India - growing up to 5 meters per year! The wood is used for making drums (like the dhol). Ancient texts mention it as a cure for many diseases.

state_flower.name: Lady's Slipper Orchid
state_flower.funfact: This orchid traps insects inside its slipper-shaped pouch to ensure pollination! Meghalaya has over 300 orchid species. The Lady's Slipper is so valuable that it's heavily protected from illegal collection.

notable_wildlife.name: Asian Elephant
notable_wildlife.funfact: Meghalaya's Garo Hills have India's largest elephant population outside protected areas! Elephants use the same migration routes their ancestors used for centuries. They can communicate through infrasound over 10 km.

notable_wildlife.name: Hoolock Gibbon
notable_wildlife.funfact: Meghalaya is a stronghold for India's only ape! Their morning songs establish territory and strengthen pair bonds. Unlike monkeys, they have no tail. They can brachiate (swing) through trees faster than a human can run.

notable_wildlife.name: Red Panda
notable_wildlife.funfact: Red pandas spend up to 13 hours a day eating bamboo because it's so low in nutrition! They have a false 'thumb' (extended wrist bone) to grip bamboo. They're not related to giant pandas - more closely related to raccoons.

notable_wildlife.name: Golden Cat
notable_wildlife.funfact: Also called 'Fire Tiger' by locals! Despite the name, they come in many colors - gold, gray, cinnamon, and even melanistic (black). They're rarely photographed in the wild.

notable_wildlife.name: Capped Langur
notable_wildlife.funfact: Named for the cap-like hair on their head! Babies are bright orange for the first few months, then turn gray-black. They can digest mature leaves that other monkeys can't, thanks to special stomach bacteria.

unique_ecosystems.name: Living Root Bridges
unique_ecosystems.funfact: The Khasi people train rubber tree roots to grow into bridges that can span 30 meters and support 50+ people! These living bridges strengthen with age and last for centuries. Some are 500+ years old and still growing!

unique_ecosystems.name: Sacred Groves (Law Kyntang)
unique_ecosystems.funfact: Meghalaya has over 100 sacred groves protected by tribal traditions! These untouched forests are biodiversity hotspots. Mawphlang Sacred Grove has 400+ tree species in just 78 hectares!

cave_fauna.name: Cave Fish (Schistura larketensis)
cave_fauna.funfact: Blind fish found only in Meghalaya caves! They evolved without eyes because there's no light. They navigate using other senses. Discovered in Siju Cave.

cave_fauna.name: Cave Crab
cave_fauna.funfact: Several endemic cave crab species exist only in Meghalaya's caves! They're pale white due to lack of sunlight.

caves_and_underground_fauna.funfact: Meghalaya has India's longest and deepest caves! Krem Liat Prah is 31 km long. These caves harbor unique species found nowhere else on Earth.

national_parks.name: Nokrek National Park
national_parks.key_species: Red Panda, Clouded Leopard, Asian Elephant, Hoolock Gibbon
national_parks.funfact: A UNESCO Biosphere Reserve! Home to the 'Citrus Gene Sanctuary' protecting wild citrus species - ancestors of all modern oranges and lemons! Nokrek is the only place in India where wild citrus diversity is protected.

national_parks.name: Balpakram National Park
national_parks.key_species: Tiger, Elephant, Red Panda, Clouded Leopard
national_parks.funfact: Known as 'Land of Spirits' or 'Land of Perpetual Winds'! The Garo tribe believes this is where souls of the dead rest before moving to the afterlife. It has dramatic canyon landscapes and waterfalls.

state_animal.name: Serow (Himalayan Serow)
state_animal.funfact: Serows are called 'goat-antelopes' because they look like a mix of both! They can scale near-vertical cliffs. They're so sure-footed that they can leap from boulder to boulder without slipping. They live alone or in pairs, unlike most goat-relatives.

state_bird.name: Mrs. Hume's Pheasant (Vavu)
state_bird.funfact: One of the most beautiful pheasants in the world! Males have iridescent steel-blue plumage and a tail up to 90cm long. They're so shy that they'll run and hide rather than fly when disturbed.

state_tree.name: Herhse (Iron Wood)
state_tree.funfact: Iron Wood is so dense it sinks in water! The wood is almost impossible to saw with hand tools. The tree produces beautiful fragrant white flowers that bloom at night. It can live for over 1,000 years.

state_flower.name: Red Vanda (Senhri)
state_flower.funfact: One of the rarest orchids in India! The stunning red flowers can have up to 50 blooms on a single plant. It grows on tree trunks and gets nutrients from air and rain. Highly endangered due to collection.

notable_wildlife.name: Hoolock Gibbon
notable_wildlife.funfact: Mizoram's forests echo with gibbon songs at dawn! These apes mate for life and raise only one baby every 2-3 years. If a partner dies, the survivor may never mate again. Their arms are longer than their legs.

notable_wildlife.name: Slow Loris
notable_wildlife.funfact: The world's only venomous primate! They lick venom glands on their elbows and deliver toxic bites. They move so slowly that algae grows on their fur. They're critically threatened by the illegal pet trade.

notable_wildlife.name: Leaf Deer
notable_wildlife.funfact: One of the smallest deer in the world! Discovered only in 1999. They're so tiny they can hide behind large leaves. Males have small antlers and vampire-like fangs (tusks).

notable_wildlife.name: Marbled Cat
notable_wildlife.funfact: One of the most mysterious cats in Asia! Rarely photographed, little is known about their behavior. Their marbled coat provides excellent camouflage. They have exceptionally long tails for balance while climbing.

bamboo_flowering_phenomenon.name: Mautam
bamboo_flowering_phenomenon.funfact: Every 48 years, bamboo forests flower and die simultaneously! This causes a massive rat population explosion (they eat the seeds), followed by famine as rats destroy crops. The last Mautam was in 2007.

national_parks.name: Murlen National Park
national_parks.key_species: Tiger, Leopard, Serow, Hoolock Gibbon
national_parks.funfact: Named after the Murlen village. It's one of the most remote parks in India! Has pristine forests with over 150 orchid species. The Murlen cliff face rises 1,500 meters and is visible from Myanmar.

national_parks.name: Phawngpui National Park (Blue Mountain)
national_parks.key_species: Serow, Barking Deer, Leopard, Red Panda
national_parks.funfact: Contains Mizoram's highest peak (2,157m)! Called 'Blue Mountain' because it appears blue from a distance. Home to rare orchids and rhododendrons. The mountain is sacred to the Mizo people.

unique_flora.name: Mizoram Pitcher Plant
unique_flora.funfact: India's only pitcher plant species! Found only in Northeast India. It traps insects in its pitcher-shaped leaves and digests them for nutrients. Can grow up to 15 meters as a climbing vine!

state_animal.name: Mithun (Gayal)
state_animal.funfact: Mithuns are sacred to Naga tribes and used as currency! Bride prices and fines are calculated in mithuns. A wealthy man might own 50+ mithuns. They're semi-domesticated but roam freely in forests.

state_bird.name: Blyth's Tragopan
state_bird.funfact: Males have spectacular blue facial skin that inflates during courtship! They perform elaborate dances, spreading their wings and fanning their tail. They're the state bird of Arunachal Pradesh too.

state_tree.name: Alder (Uttis)
state_tree.funfact: Alder trees fix nitrogen in soil, making them essential for jhum (slash-and-burn) cultivation recovery! They're often the first trees to colonize bare land. Their wood is used for making traditional Naga drums.

state_flower.name: Rhododendron
state_flower.funfact: Nagaland has over 30 rhododendron species! When they bloom, entire mountainsides turn red and pink. The flower juice is used to make a traditional drink. Bees that feed on rhododendrons produce 'mad honey' that can cause hallucinations!

notable_wildlife.name: Hoolock Gibbon
notable_wildlife.funfact: Nagas have traditionally protected gibbons because of their human-like appearance. Gibbons can swing through trees at 55 km/h. Their morning songs can be heard from 2 km away!

notable_wildlife.name: Amur Falcon
notable_wildlife.funfact: Over 1 MILLION Amur Falcons stop in Nagaland during their 22,000 km migration from Siberia to South Africa! It's one of nature's greatest spectacles. Nagaland was once a hunting ground but is now a protected stopover.

notable_wildlife.name: Serow
notable_wildlife.funfact: Serows can climb nearly vertical cliffs! They live alone and are extremely territorial. They mark their territory with scent glands below their eyes. Despite looking clumsy, they're incredibly agile.

notable_wildlife.name: Binturong (Bearcat)
notable_wildlife.funfact: Binturongs smell like buttered popcorn! This scent comes from a chemical also found in popcorn. They're one of only two carnivores with a prehensile tail (the other is the kinkajou). They can rotate their hind legs backward.

hornbill_significance.species_found: Great Hornbill, Wreathed Hornbill, Rufous-necked Hornbill
hornbill_significance.funfact: Hornbill feathers are essential for traditional Naga warrior headdresses. The Hornbill Festival celebrates Naga culture! Nagas now protect hornbills - killing one brings shame to the entire village.

national_parks.name: Intanki National Park
national_parks.key_species: Elephant, Hoolock Gibbon, Binturong, Serow
national_parks.funfact: Created to protect the endangered hoolock gibbon! One of the most remote parks in India. The forest is so dense that satellite mapping is difficult. Has over 300 bird species including all 4 hornbill species found in the region.

conservation_success.funfact: Until 2012, Nagas hunted Amur Falcons for food. After awareness campaigns, they became protectors! The transformation was so remarkable that it won the 'India Biodiversity Award'. Villages now compete to host the falcons.

state_animal.name: Sambar Deer
state_animal.funfact: Sambar is India's largest deer, weighing up to 300 kg! When alarmed, they make a distinctive 'dhank' call that alerts all forest animals. Tigers find them difficult prey because of their size and powerful kicks.

state_bird.name: Indian Roller (Nilakantha)
state_bird.funfact: Called 'Nilakantha' (Blue Throat) after Lord Shiva in Odisha! They perform spectacular rolling flights during courtship. Seeing one during Dussehra is considered auspicious.

state_tree.name: Sacred Fig (Ashwatha)
state_tree.funfact: Buddha attained enlightenment under a Sacred Fig (Bodhi Tree)! These trees release oxygen 24/7 (unlike most trees). They can live for 3,000+ years. Konark Sun Temple has carvings of this tree.

state_flower.name: Ashoka (Ashok)
state_flower.funfact: 'Ashoka' means 'without sorrow' - the tree is believed to remove grief! In Ramayana, Sita was held captive in an Ashoka grove. The bark is used in traditional medicine for women's health for over 3,000 years.

notable_wildlife.name: Olive Ridley Sea Turtle
notable_wildlife.funfact: Gahirmatha hosts the world's largest Olive Ridley rookery! Over 500,000 turtles arrive simultaneously for mass nesting (arribada). Females return to the same beach where they were born 15-30 years earlier!

notable_wildlife.name: Saltwater Crocodile
notable_wildlife.funfact: Bhitarkanika has India's largest saltwater crocodiles - some over 6 meters (20 feet)! They're the world's largest living reptiles. They can swim 1,000 km in open ocean. One crocodile killed here measured 7.1 meters!

notable_wildlife.name: Indian Python
notable_wildlife.funfact: Indian pythons can swallow prey larger than their head by dislocating their jaw! They can go without food for 2 years. A female can lay 100 eggs and coils around them for 2-3 months, not eating the entire time.

notable_wildlife.name: Irrawaddy Dolphin
notable_wildlife.funfact: Chilika Lake has the largest population of Irrawaddy dolphins in India! They have a distinctive rounded head with no beak. They can spit water to herd fish - the only dolphins known to do this!

notable_wildlife.name: Fishing Cat
notable_wildlife.funfact: Unlike most cats, fishing cats love water! They have partially webbed paws for swimming. They dive into water to catch fish and can swim long distances. They've been seen eating crocodile eggs!

endemic_species.name: White Tiger (Rare genetic variant)
endemic_species.funfact: All captive white tigers in the world descend from 'Mohan', a white tiger captured in Odisha in 1951! White tigers are not albinos - they have a genetic mutation affecting coat color. They're extinct in the wild.

national_parks.name: Simlipal National Park
national_parks.key_species: Tiger, Elephant, Gaur, Mugger Crocodile
national_parks.funfact: Named after the Simul (silk cotton) trees! One of India's largest tiger reserves. Has spectacular waterfalls including Barehipani (399m) and Joranda (181m). Melanistic (black) tigers have been photographed here!

national_parks.name: Bhitarkanika National Park
national_parks.key_species: Saltwater Crocodile, Water Monitor, King Cobra
national_parks.funfact: Has India's second-largest mangrove forest! Home to the largest saltwater crocodiles in India. Over 1 million Olive Ridley turtles nest at nearby Gahirmatha beach - the world's largest rookery!

chilika_lake.name: Chilika Lake
chilika_lake.funfact: Over 1 million migratory birds visit Chilika annually from as far as Siberia! It's India's largest coastal lagoon. The Nalabana Island inside the lake is a bird sanctuary hosting over 400,000 birds at peak season!

state_animal.name: Blackbuck
state_animal.funfact: Blackbucks are protected by the Bishnoi community who consider them sacred! In 1998, Salman Khan was prosecuted for allegedly killing blackbucks in Rajasthan - the case lasted 20 years. Males turn jet black during breeding season.

state_bird.name: Northern Goshawk (Baaz)
state_bird.funfact: The Baaz (hawk) is the emblem of the Sikh faith! Guru Gobind Singh kept hawks and established falconry traditions. They can chase prey through dense forests at 60 km/h, weaving between trees with incredible agility.

state_tree.name: Shisham (Indian Rosewood)
state_tree.funfact: Shisham wood is so valuable it's called 'Punjab Teak'! It's used to make premium furniture and musical instruments. The wood becomes more beautiful with age. It fixes nitrogen, improving soil fertility.

state_flower.name: Gladiolus
state_flower.funfact: Punjab is India's largest gladiolus producer! A single stem can have 20+ flowers. The name comes from Latin 'gladius' (sword) due to sword-shaped leaves. Punjab's flower markets export millions of gladiolus stems annually.

notable_wildlife.name: Nilgai (Blue Bull)
notable_wildlife.funfact: Asia's largest antelope! Males have blue-gray coats (hence 'Nilgai' - blue cow). They're often seen in agricultural areas. Despite weighing 300 kg, they can jump 1.5-meter fences from a standing position!

notable_wildlife.name: Indian Hare
notable_wildlife.funfact: Indian hares can run at 70 km/h! Unlike rabbits, they don't dig burrows - babies are born in shallow depressions. They can leap 3 meters in a single bound. Their large ears help regulate body temperature.

notable_wildlife.name: Sarus Crane
notable_wildlife.funfact: The world's tallest flying bird at 1.8 meters! Sarus cranes mate for life and dance together. If one partner dies, the other often stops eating and dies of grief. They're considered symbols of marital fidelity in India.

notable_wildlife.name: Indian Peafowl
notable_wildlife.funfact: India's national bird is common in Punjab! A peacock's train has about 200 feathers with 'eye' spots that appear to stare at predators. They eat snakes and are protected by farmers for pest control.

wildlife_sanctuaries.name: Harike Wetland and Bird Sanctuary
wildlife_sanctuaries.funfact: North India's largest wetland! Created at the confluence of Sutlej and Beas rivers. Hosts over 1 million migratory birds in winter including rare Dalmatian Pelicans. It's a Ramsar Wetland of International Importance.

wildlife_sanctuaries.name: Bir Moti Bagh Sanctuary
wildlife_sanctuaries.funfact: Located within Patiala city! Once the hunting ground of Maharajas of Patiala. One of India's smallest sanctuaries but important for educating urban populations about wildlife.

agricultural_biodiversity.fact: Punjab is India's breadbasket but intensive farming has reduced biodiversity
agricultural_biodiversity.conservation_efforts: Movement to restore natural grasslands and wetlands
agricultural_biodiversity.funfact: Once, Punjab's plains had lions, cheetahs, and wild elephants! Old hunting records show incredible biodiversity that existed before intensive agriculture.

state_animal.name: Chinkara (Indian Gazelle)
state_animal.funfact: Chinkaras can survive without drinking water for their entire life! They get all moisture from plants and morning dew. They can run at 80 km/h and leap 6 meters. The name comes from the sneezing 'chink' sound they make.

state_bird.name: Great Indian Bustard
state_bird.funfact: Fewer than 150 Great Indian Bustards survive! Once considered for National Bird of India but rejected because its name sounds like a curse word. Males perform spectacular displays, inflating their neck like a balloon.

state_tree.name: Khejri
state_tree.funfact: The Bishnoi community sacrificed their lives protecting Khejri trees in 1730! Amrita Devi and 363 villagers were killed for hugging trees. This led to India's first wildlife protection act. The pods (sangri) are a Rajasthani delicacy.

state_flower.name: Rohida (Desert Teak)
state_flower.funfact: Rohida flowers in the driest months when nothing else blooms! The orange-red flowers carpet the desert floor. It's the only timber tree that survives in the Thar Desert. The tree can live for 200+ years.

notable_wildlife.name: Bengal Tiger
notable_wildlife.funfact: Ranthambore's tigress 'Machali' (T-16) was the most photographed tiger in history! She appeared in National Geographic documentaries and killed a 14-foot crocodile. She lived to 19 years - ancient for a wild tiger.

notable_wildlife.name: Indian Leopard
notable_wildlife.funfact: Jawai region has the highest concentration of leopards living alongside humans in India! Leopards here live in caves and rocky outcrops. They've learned to coexist with local Rabari shepherds who don't fear them.

notable_wildlife.name: Indian Wolf
notable_wildlife.funfact: Rajasthan has India's largest wolf population! They hunt in small packs of 3-5 and can take down nilgai. The 'werewolf' legend may have originated from Indian wolves that occasionally attacked children.

notable_wildlife.name: Caracal
notable_wildlife.funfact: Caracals can leap 3 meters into the air to catch flying birds! Their tufted ears help them hear prey in tall grass. They were trained by Mughal emperors for hunting birds. They can take down prey twice their size.

notable_wildlife.name: Desert Fox
notable_wildlife.funfact: Desert foxes have enormous ears (10cm) that radiate heat and help them hear prey underground! They can survive body temperatures of 40°C. They live in burrows during the day and hunt at night.

notable_wildlife.name: Spiny-tailed Lizard
notable_wildlife.funfact: Also called 'Sanda' - locals believe its oil has medicinal properties (it doesn't)! They can change color based on temperature. They dig burrows up to 2 meters deep. Sadly, thousands are killed for traditional medicine.

national_parks.name: Ranthambore National Park
national_parks.key_species: Tiger, Leopard, Sloth Bear, Sambar
national_parks.funfact: One of the best places in the world to see wild tigers! Has a 1000-year-old fort inside the park. Tigers here are unusually bold, often walking on safari roads in daylight. The legendary tigress Machali ruled for 15 years.

national_parks.name: Keoladeo National Park (Bharatpur)
national_parks.key_species: Over 350 bird species, including formerly Siberian Cranes
national_parks.funfact: UNESCO World Heritage Site! Once a royal hunting ground where British Viceroys shot 4,000 ducks in a single day! Now India's premier bird sanctuary. Siberian Cranes used to winter here but haven't been seen since 2002.

national_parks.name: Desert National Park
national_parks.key_species: Great Indian Bustard, Chinkara, Desert Fox, Spiny-tailed Lizard
national_parks.funfact: One of the largest national parks in India! Contains 180-million-year-old fossils including dinosaur bones. It's the last refuge of the critically endangered Great Indian Bustard. Has sand dunes up to 50 meters high!

desert_ecosystem.name: Thar Desert
desert_ecosystem.funfact: The Thar is the world's most densely populated desert! It supports 83 mammal species, 355 bird species, and 110 reptile species. Despite receiving less than 25cm rain annually, it sustains 40 million people.

state_animal.name: Red Panda
state_animal.funfact: Red pandas spend up to 13 hours a day eating bamboo because it's so low in nutrition! They have a 'false thumb' (extended wrist bone) to grip bamboo. They're not related to giant pandas - more closely related to raccoons and weasels.

state_bird.name: Blood Pheasant
state_bird.habitat: High altitude forests and alpine meadows
state_bird.funfact: Named for the blood-red streaks on male's feathers! They live at elevations up to 5,000 meters. In winter, they descend to lower altitudes. They're the only pheasant that lives in the alpine zone year-round.

state_tree.name: Rhododendron
state_tree.funfact: Sikkim has over 36 rhododendron species! When they bloom in spring, entire mountains turn red, pink, and white. The flowers are used to make juice and wine. Some species grow as tall trees (30+ meters)!

state_flower.name: Noble Dendrobium Orchid
state_flower.funfact: Sikkim has over 550 orchid species - 28% of India's total! This orchid is called 'Donpang' locally. Each flower can last 2-3 weeks. It grows on tree branches without harming the host tree.

notable_wildlife.name: Snow Leopard
notable_wildlife.funfact: Snow leopards can leap 15 meters in a single bound! Their tail is almost as long as their body and so thick they use it as a blanket. They can take down prey 3 times their weight. Fewer than 7,000 remain in the wild.

notable_wildlife.name: Himalayan Black Bear
notable_wildlife.funfact: They have a distinctive white 'V' on their chest! Despite being called 'black bear', their coat can be brown or reddish. They're excellent climbers and build nests in trees for sleeping. They can eat 20,000 ants in one sitting!

notable_wildlife.name: Himalayan Monal
notable_wildlife.funfact: Males have 9 different metallic colors that shimmer in sunlight - they're living rainbows! They dig through snow to find food. Their call echoes through mountains and sounds like a squeaky door.

notable_wildlife.name: Tibetan Wolf
notable_wildlife.funfact: Tibetan wolves are larger than Indian wolves and can survive at 5,000+ meters! They have thicker fur for extreme cold. They hunt blue sheep and marmots. They're genetically distinct from other wolf subspecies.

notable_wildlife.name: Blue Sheep (Bharal)
notable_wildlife.funfact: Neither truly sheep nor goat - they're an evolutionary link between both! They can navigate near-vertical cliffs with ease. Their blue-gray coat provides perfect camouflage against rocky slopes. Snow leopards depend on them as primary prey.

notable_wildlife.name: Himalayan Marmot
notable_wildlife.funfact: These giant squirrel-relatives hibernate for 8 months! They can survive at altitudes above 5,000m. They whistle loudly to warn of predators - earning the name 'whistling marmot'. Entire families cuddle together in underground burrows.

notable_wildlife.name: Satyr Tragopan
notable_wildlife.funfact: Males have electric blue facial skin and a blue inflatable throat lappet! During courtship, they fan their feathers and inflate colorful throat patches. They're named after Satyrs from Greek mythology due to their wild appearance.

endemic_species.name: Sikkim Stag Beetle
endemic_species.funfact: Male stag beetles have massive mandibles (jaws) that look like deer antlers! They use them to fight rivals. Despite fierce appearance, they only drink tree sap. Larvae live in rotting wood for 3-5 years before becoming adults.

national_parks.name: Khangchendzonga National Park
national_parks.key_species: Snow Leopard, Red Panda, Himalayan Black Bear, Musk Deer
