national_parks.funfact: UNESCO World Heritage Site! Contains India's highest peak (Khangchendzonga at 8,586m). Has glaciers, alpine meadows, and ancient forests in one park. Sacred to both Buddhists and Hindus. Over 550 bird species recorded!

orchid_biodiversity.funfact: Sikkim is called 'Paradise of Orchids'! The entire state is an orchid hotspot. Some orchids here bloom only once every 12 years. Many species are endemic - found nowhere else on Earth.

butterfly_diversity.funfact: Sikkim has more butterfly species than all of Europe combined! The Kaiser-i-Hind ('Emperor of India') is so rare and beautiful that collectors pay thousands of dollars for specimens. It's now strictly protected.

state_animal.name: Nilgiri Tahr
state_animal.funfact: Found ONLY in the Western Ghats! Adult males have a distinctive silvery 'saddle' marking on their back. They can navigate near-vertical cliffs with rubbery hooves that grip like suction cups. Only about 2,500 remain in the wild.

state_bird.name: Emerald Dove
state_bird.funfact: Their wings shimmer with emerald-green iridescence! They're ground feeders but sleep in trees. Unlike most doves, they're extremely shy and rarely seen despite being common. They make a deep, mournful 'coo' at dawn and dusk.

state_tree.name: Palmyra Palm (Panai)
state_tree.uses: Food, toddy, timber, thatch - 801 uses documented!
state_tree.funfact: Tamil literature documents 801 uses for Palmyra Palm! It's called 'Karpaga Vriksham' (wish-fulfilling tree). The toddy (fermented sap) is a traditional drink. A single tree can live for 100+ years and yield products worth lakhs.

state_flower.name: Gloriosa Lily (Karthigai Poo)
state_flower.funfact: One of the most striking flowers in India! The petals curl backward like flames. Despite its beauty, it's highly poisonous - all parts can be lethal if ingested. Used in traditional medicine in tiny, controlled doses.

notable_wildlife.name: Bengal Tiger
notable_wildlife.funfact: Tamil Nadu's tiger reserves are connected to Karnataka's, forming India's largest tiger landscape! Tigers here regularly cross state borders. The Nilgiri Biosphere has over 700 tigers - one of the densest populations.

notable_wildlife.name: Asian Elephant
notable_wildlife.funfact: Tamil Nadu has one of the largest wild elephant populations! The 'Elephant Corridor' connecting Mudumalai to Bandipur is crucial. Elephants here have adapted to cross highways using underpasses built specifically for them.

notable_wildlife.name: Lion-tailed Macaque
notable_wildlife.funfact: One of the world's rarest monkeys with only 4,000 remaining! They have a magnificent silver mane. They spend 95% of their life in trees. They communicate using 17 different calls. Some populations haven't been on the ground in generations!

notable_wildlife.name: Grizzled Giant Squirrel
notable_wildlife.location: Srivilliputhur and Grizzled Squirrel Wildlife Sanctuary
notable_wildlife.funfact: Found only in South India and Sri Lanka! They're called 'grizzled' because of their salt-and-pepper colored fur. They build multiple basketball-sized nests in a single tree. They can leap 6 meters between branches!

notable_wildlife.name: Nilgiri Marten
notable_wildlife.funfact: South India's only marten species and extremely rare! They have a distinctive yellow-orange throat patch. They can rotate their hind feet 180° to climb down trees headfirst. Active both day and night.

notable_wildlife.name: Dugong (Sea Cow)
notable_wildlife.funfact: India's only remaining dugong population lives in Tamil Nadu's Gulf of Mannar! They're the only herbivorous marine mammals. They 'mow' seagrass meadows, grazing like underwater cows. Sailors' sightings inspired mermaid legends!

notable_wildlife.name: Whale Shark
notable_wildlife.funfact: The world's largest fish can grow to 12 meters! Despite their size, they eat only plankton and small fish. Each whale shark has a unique spot pattern like a fingerprint. Tamil fishermen now protect them after conservation programs.

endemic_species.name: Anamalai Flying Frog
endemic_species.funfact: These frogs 'fly' by gliding up to 12 meters using webbed feet as parachutes! They lay eggs on branches overhanging water - tadpoles fall into the water when they hatch. Found only in a tiny area of Anamalai Hills.

endemic_species.name: Nilgiri Blue Robin
endemic_species.funfact: Found only in the shola-grassland ecosystem of Tamil Nadu and Kerala! Males have stunning cobalt-blue plumage. They're extremely secretive, hopping through dense undergrowth. Climate change threatens their high-altitude habitat.

national_parks.name: Mudumalai National Park
national_parks.key_species: Tiger, Elephant, Gaur, Wild Dog, Leopard
national_parks.funfact: Part of the Nilgiri Biosphere Reserve - India's first biosphere reserve! Located at the junction of three states. Has India's largest elephant camp at Theppakadu. The 'Moyar Gorge' here is a spectacular canyon.

national_parks.name: Anamalai Tiger Reserve (Indira Gandhi Wildlife Sanctuary)
national_parks.key_species: Tiger, Lion-tailed Macaque, Nilgiri Tahr, Elephant
national_parks.funfact: Has one of the highest tiger densities in South India! Contains 'Grass Hills' - a unique rolling grassland ecosystem. The sanctuary spans from 340m to 2,400m altitude, creating multiple climate zones.

national_parks.name: Gulf of Mannar Marine National Park
national_parks.key_species: Dugong, Sea Turtle, Dolphins, Coral Reefs
national_parks.funfact: India's first marine biosphere reserve! Has 21 islands with coral reefs, seagrass beds, and mangroves. Contains 3,600+ plant and animal species. The 'Adam's Bridge' (Ram Setu) chain of islands starts here!

point_calimere.name: Point Calimere Wildlife Sanctuary
point_calimere.funfact: Hosts over 100,000 flamingos during winter! It's a crucial stopover for migratory birds from Siberia and Europe. The salt pans and mudflats attract 257 bird species. Blackbucks graze alongside flamingos!

state_animal.name: Spotted Deer (Chital)
state_animal.funfact: Chital are the most common deer in Indian forests! Their spotted coat provides excellent camouflage. They often follow langurs and pick up fruits the monkeys drop. They alarm-call when they spot predators, warning other animals.

state_bird.name: Indian Roller (Palapitta)
state_bird.funfact: Called 'Neelakantha' (Blue-throated) after Lord Shiva! They perform spectacular rolling aerial acrobatics during courtship - diving, twisting, and somersaulting. Their feathers display 8 different shades of blue.

state_tree.name: Jammi (Prosopis)
state_tree.funfact: Jammi tree is sacred to Telangana! During Dussehra, people worship the Jammi tree. Legend says the Pandavas hid their weapons in a Jammi tree during exile. The tree can survive extreme drought and fix nitrogen in soil.

state_flower.name: Tangedu (Senna auriculata)
state_flower.funfact: Tangedu has bright yellow flowers used in Bathukamma festival! Women create elaborate floral stacks for this unique Telangana festival. The plant has medicinal properties and is used in traditional diabetes treatment.

notable_wildlife.name: Bengal Tiger
notable_wildlife.funfact: Tigers are making a comeback in Telangana! After decades of absence, tigers have been confirmed in both reserves. The Godavari and Krishna river basins provide crucial corridors for tiger movement between states.

notable_wildlife.name: Indian Leopard
notable_wildlife.funfact: Leopards in Telangana often live near human settlements! They've learned to hunt at night and hide during the day. Camera traps have revealed surprisingly high numbers even near Hyderabad city.

notable_wildlife.name: Sloth Bear
notable_wildlife.funfact: Sloth bears can close their nostrils completely to vacuum up termites! They carry cubs on their back - the only bear species to do this. A single bear can destroy a termite mound in minutes and eat 10,000 termites.

notable_wildlife.name: Indian Wolf
notable_wildlife.funfact: Telangana's grasslands still support Indian wolves! They're smaller than their northern relatives but equally social. They hunt cooperatively and share food with pack members. They were likely the 'wolves' in Kipling's Jungle Book.

notable_wildlife.name: Indian Pangolin
notable_wildlife.funfact: The world's most trafficked mammal! Pangolins are covered in scales made of keratin (like fingernails). When threatened, they curl into a ball so tight that even tigers can't unroll them. One pangolin eats 70 million ants per year!

notable_wildlife.name: Mugger Crocodile
notable_wildlife.funfact: Muggers live in surprising places - even urban lakes near Hyderabad! They've been spotted in Hussain Sagar Lake in the city center. They can survive in polluted water and eat almost anything including garbage.

endemic_species.name: Deccan Mahseer
endemic_species.funfact: Called the 'Tiger of Rivers'! Once abundant, now rare due to pollution and dams. They can grow to 45 kg and live for 25 years. Considered sacred fish in some communities and not eaten.

wildlife_sanctuaries.name: Kawal Tiger Reserve
wildlife_sanctuaries.key_species: Tiger, Leopard, Sloth Bear, Wild Dog
wildlife_sanctuaries.funfact: Telangana's first tiger reserve! The forests are so dense that tiger surveys are extremely challenging. Connected to Maharashtra's tiger landscapes, allowing genetic exchange between populations.

wildlife_sanctuaries.name: Amrabad Tiger Reserve
wildlife_sanctuaries.funfact: India's second-largest tiger reserve! Located in the Nallamala Hills. Has ancient Buddhist sites within the reserve. The Nagarjunasagar-Srisailam forests form a crucial wildlife corridor.

wildlife_sanctuaries.name: Pocharam Wildlife Sanctuary
wildlife_sanctuaries.funfact: Built around Pocharam Lake! One of the oldest protected areas in Telangana. Popular for bird watching with over 200 species. The lake attracts thousands of migratory birds in winter.

unique_ecosystem.name: Deccan Plateau Dry Deciduous Forests
unique_ecosystem.characteristics: Thorny scrub, dry deciduous trees, rocky outcrops
unique_ecosystem.funfact: These forests look barren but support incredible biodiversity! They're home to unique reptiles and insects found nowhere else. The rocky hillocks (inselbergs) host endemic plants that evolved in isolation.

state_animal.name: Phayre's Langur (Spectacled Langur)
state_animal.funfact: Named for British administrator Arthur Phayre! They have distinctive white rings around their eyes like spectacles. Babies are born bright orange and slowly turn gray. They can digest mature leaves that other monkeys can't.

state_bird.name: Green Imperial Pigeon
state_bird.funfact: These large pigeons are crucial for forest regeneration! They eat fruits whole and disperse seeds far from parent trees. Despite being nearly 50cm long, they're incredibly agile fliers. Their cooing echoes through forests.

state_tree.name: Agar (Agarwood)
state_tree.funfact: Agarwood is one of the most expensive woods in the world - worth more than gold by weight! The fragrant resin only forms when the tree is infected by mold. A kilogram of high-quality agarwood can cost $100,000!

state_flower.name: Nageshwar (Mesua ferrea)
state_flower.funfact: The flowers are so fragrant they perfume entire forests! The wood is called 'Iron Wood' because it's so dense it sinks in water. Ancient temples were built using this wood. The flowers bloom at night and emit stronger fragrance.

notable_wildlife.name: Clouded Leopard
notable_wildlife.funfact: Tripura has declared the Clouded Leopard its state animal since 2007! They have the longest canine teeth relative to body size of any cat. They can climb down trees headfirst and hang from branches by their hind legs.

notable_wildlife.name: Hoolock Gibbon
notable_wildlife.funfact: India's only ape! Their morning songs establish territory and can be heard 2 km away. Couples 'duet' together. They swing through trees at 55 km/h and rarely touch the ground. They mate for life.

notable_wildlife.name: Bison (Gaur)
notable_wildlife.funfact: The world's largest wild cattle! Bulls can weigh 1,500 kg. Despite their massive size, they can jump 2-meter fences. Even tigers rarely attack healthy adult gaurs. They have a distinctive muscular ridge on their back.

notable_wildlife.name: Capped Langur
notable_wildlife.funfact: Named for the cap-like hair on their head! Newborns are golden-orange for the first few months before turning gray. They have multi-chambered stomachs like cows, allowing them to digest tough leaves.

notable_wildlife.name: Indian Python
notable_wildlife.funfact: Indian pythons can go without food for up to 2 years! They have heat-sensing pits to detect warm-blooded prey. A female can lay 100 eggs and coils around them for 2-3 months without eating.

national_parks.name: Clouded Leopard National Park
national_parks.key_species: Clouded Leopard, Binturong, Slow Loris
national_parks.funfact: India's smallest national park dedicated to a single species! It's essentially a breeding center for clouded leopards. The elusive cats are rarely seen in the wild, so captive breeding is crucial for conservation.

wildlife_sanctuaries.name: Sepahijala Wildlife Sanctuary
wildlife_sanctuaries.key_species: Spectacled Langur, Capped Langur, Clouded Leopard
wildlife_sanctuaries.funfact: Famous for its primates! Has a natural lake with a rubber tree-lined shore. The sanctuary has a zoo and botanical garden alongside natural forest. One of the best places to see spectacled langurs.

wildlife_sanctuaries.name: Gumti Wildlife Sanctuary
wildlife_sanctuaries.funfact: The largest wildlife sanctuary in Tripura! Has ancient temples within the forest. The Gumti River flows through, creating diverse habitats. Elephants here migrate between India and Bangladesh.

rubber_plantation_wildlife.fact: Tripura has extensive rubber plantations that host surprising wildlife
rubber_plantation_wildlife.funfact: Clouded leopards, civets, and langurs have adapted to live in rubber plantations! The trees provide cover and food. Conservation efforts now integrate rubber farming with wildlife protection.

state_animal.name: Swamp Deer (Barasingha)
state_animal.funfact: 'Barasingha' means '12-tined' referring to the antlers! The UP subspecies lives in tall grasslands, not swamps. Only about 3,000 remain in the wild. They were nearly extinct but conservation efforts in Dudhwa saved them.

state_bird.name: Sarus Crane
state_bird.funfact: The world's tallest flying bird at 1.8 meters! Sarus cranes mate for life and perform elaborate synchronized dances. If one partner dies, the other often stops eating. UP has India's largest Sarus population.

state_tree.name: Ashoka
state_tree.funfact: 'Ashoka' means 'without sorrow'! In the Ramayana, Sita was held in an Ashoka grove. The bark is used in traditional medicine for women's health. The tree is considered sacred to both Hindus and Buddhists.

state_flower.name: Palash (Flame of the Forest)
state_flower.funfact: When Palash blooms, forests look like they're on fire! The flowers are used to make natural Holi colors. They were used to dye clothes for thousands of years. The tree is also called 'Dhak' and features in countless poems.

notable_wildlife.name: Bengal Tiger
notable_wildlife.funfact: UP's Dudhwa is one of India's finest tiger reserves! It's the only place where Indian tigers and Indian rhinos coexist naturally. The grasslands here are so tall that elephants can disappear in them!

notable_wildlife.name: Indian One-horned Rhinoceros
notable_wildlife.funfact: Rhinos were reintroduced to UP's Dudhwa from Assam in the 1980s! They were extinct here for over 100 years. Now about 40 rhinos live in Dudhwa. Their armor-like skin has folds but is surprisingly soft!

notable_wildlife.name: Gangetic Dolphin
notable_wildlife.funfact: India's National Aquatic Animal! These dolphins are effectively blind - they navigate using echolocation in muddy water. They swim on their side and can only live in freshwater. UP's Chambal has a significant population.

notable_wildlife.name: Gharial
notable_wildlife.funfact: Gharials have the narrowest snout of any crocodilian! Males develop a bulbous 'ghara' (pot) on their snout. They're fish specialists and can't walk on land like other crocodiles. Only about 650 adults survive.

notable_wildlife.name: Indian Wolf
notable_wildlife.funfact: UP's wolves inspired Kipling's 'The Jungle Book'! They're smaller than northern wolves but equally intelligent. They hunt in packs of 3-5. They've been known to raid villages for livestock.

notable_wildlife.name: Blackbuck
notable_wildlife.funfact: Once abundant, blackbucks now survive mainly in protected areas. Males turn jet black during breeding season. They can run at 80 km/h and leap 2 meters high. The spiral horns can grow to 70cm!

national_parks.name: Dudhwa National Park
national_parks.key_species: Tiger, Rhino, Swamp Deer, Elephant
national_parks.funfact: The ONLY place in India where tigers and rhinos coexist! Has tall elephant grass that can hide entire elephant herds. The park borders Nepal. Famous conservationist Billy Arjan Singh lived and worked here.

national_parks.name: Pilibhit Tiger Reserve
national_parks.key_species: Tiger, Leopard, Swamp Deer, Fishing Cat
national_parks.funfact: Won the TX2 Award for doubling its tiger population! The reserve has flooded grasslands and sal forests. Connected to Nepal's forests, allowing transboundary tiger movement. Has over 130 bird species.

chambal_sanctuary.name: National Chambal Sanctuary
chambal_sanctuary.key_species: Gharial, Gangetic Dolphin, Mugger Crocodile, Red-crowned Turtle
chambal_sanctuary.funfact: The cleanest river in North India! Supports one of the last healthy gharial populations. The river is so pristine because dacoits (bandits) kept people away for decades. Now it's a conservation success!

state_animal.name: Alpine Musk Deer
state_animal.funfact: Male musk deer have vampire-like fangs (tusks) up to 7cm long! They produce musk - the world's most expensive animal product at $45,000/kg. They're heavily poached despite strict protection. They have no antlers.

state_bird.name: Himalayan Monal
state_bird.funfact: Males display 9 metallic colors that shimmer like a rainbow! They dig through snow to find food using their curved beaks. Their call echoes through the Himalayas. They're depicted on the ₹10 coin!

state_tree.name: Buransh (Rhododendron)
state_tree.funfact: When Buransh blooms, entire Himalayan hillsides turn red! The flower juice is rich in Vitamin C and made into a popular drink. The nectar produces 'mad honey' that can cause hallucinations in large doses!

state_flower.name: Brahma Kamal
state_flower.funfact: Called 'King of Himalayan Flowers'! Blooms only once a year, at night, above 4,500m altitude. According to mythology, it was used to revive Lakshmana in the Ramayana. It's the state flower of Uttarakhand.

notable_wildlife.name: Bengal Tiger
notable_wildlife.funfact: Jim Corbett was India's first national park (1936) and first tiger reserve! It's named after legendary hunter-turned-conservationist Jim Corbett. Tigers here live in hill forests - unusual for the species.

notable_wildlife.name: Asian Elephant
notable_wildlife.funfact: Uttarakhand's elephants migrate through ancient corridors connecting reserves! The Rajaji-Corbett corridor is crucial for genetic diversity. Elephants here have learned to use highway underpasses safely.

notable_wildlife.name: Snow Leopard
notable_wildlife.funfact: Snow leopards in Uttarakhand live above 3,000m! They can jump 15 meters in one leap. Their tail is so thick they wrap it around their face while sleeping. They're called 'Grey Ghosts' because they're so elusive.

notable_wildlife.name: Himalayan Black Bear
notable_wildlife.funfact: They have a distinctive white 'V' on their chest! They're excellent tree climbers. Before hibernation, they can gain 100 kg eating berries and nuts. They've attacked pilgrims on the Kedarnath trail.

notable_wildlife.name: Himalayan Tahr
notable_wildlife.funfact: These goat-antelopes have rubbery hooves that grip near-vertical cliffs! Males have magnificent manes. They can survive temperatures of -40°C. They're the primary prey of snow leopards.

notable_wildlife.name: Serow
notable_wildlife.funfact: Serows are called 'goat-antelopes'! They're solitary and extremely territorial. They can navigate near-vertical cliff faces. Despite looking clumsy, they're incredibly agile. They're most active at dawn and dusk.

endemic_species.name: Golden Mahseer
endemic_species.funfact: Called the 'Tiger of Rivers'! They can grow to 2 meters and 50 kg. They migrate upstream to spawn, leaping waterfalls like salmon. Overfishing and dams have made them rare. They're considered sacred.

national_parks.name: Jim Corbett National Park
national_parks.key_species: Tiger, Elephant, Leopard, King Cobra
national_parks.funfact: India's first national park and first tiger reserve! Named after Jim Corbett who killed man-eating tigers but later became a conservationist. The Ramganga River creates stunning grasslands. Has over 650 bird species!

national_parks.name: Rajaji National Park
national_parks.key_species: Elephant, Tiger, Leopard, King Cobra
national_parks.funfact: Named after C. Rajagopalachari! The Ganges enters the plains at Haridwar through this park. Has one of the highest elephant densities in India. The Chilla-Motichur corridor is crucial for elephant migration.

national_parks.name: Valley of Flowers National Park
national_parks.key_species: 600+ flowering plant species, Himalayan Black Bear, Snow Leopard
national_parks.funfact: UNESCO World Heritage Site! Over 600 flower species bloom simultaneously in summer - a riot of colors! It was 'discovered' by mountaineer Frank Smythe in 1931. Blue Poppy, Brahma Kamal, and rare orchids carpet the valley.

national_parks.name: Nanda Devi National Park
national_parks.key_species: Snow Leopard, Himalayan Black Bear, Musk Deer, Bharal
national_parks.funfact: UNESCO World Heritage Site! Dominated by India's second-highest peak Nanda Devi (7,816m). The inner sanctuary was closed in 1982 to preserve its pristine ecosystem. Only scientists can enter the core zone.

gangotri_ecosystem.name: Gangotri National Park
gangotri_ecosystem.funfact: Contains the Gangotri Glacier - source of the holy Ganges! The glacier has retreated 2 km in the last century due to climate change. Snow leopards, blue sheep, and Himalayan tahr live above the glaciers.

state_animal.name: Fishing Cat
state_animal.funfact: Unlike most cats, fishing cats LOVE water! They have partially webbed paws and can swim long distances. They dive into water to catch fish. They tap the water surface with their paw to mimic insect movements and attract fish!

state_bird.name: White-throated Kingfisher
state_bird.funfact: Despite being called 'kingfisher', they eat more insects and lizards than fish! They have a distinctive laughing call. They nest in tunnels dug into earthen banks. Their bright blue and chestnut plumage is unmistakable.

state_tree.name: Chatim (Devil Tree)
state_tree.funfact: Called 'Devil Tree' because of strong-smelling flowers that bloom at night! Traditional school writing boards (peti) were made from this wood - hence 'scholaris'. The bark is used to treat malaria and fever.

state_flower.name: Night-flowering Jasmine (Shiuli)
state_flower.funfact: Flowers open at dusk and fall at dawn - carpeting the ground white! The orange stalks are used as natural dye for monk robes. The name 'arbor-tristis' means 'sad tree' because flowers fall so quickly. Iconic during Durga Puja.

notable_wildlife.name: Royal Bengal Tiger
notable_wildlife.funfact: Sundarban tigers are the only ones that regularly swim in saltwater! They can swim up to 8 km and hunt in water. They're smaller than mainland tigers but exceptionally powerful. They're the only man-eating tiger population today.

notable_wildlife.name: Indian Elephant
notable_wildlife.funfact: West Bengal's elephants migrate through 'elephant corridors' connecting Bhutan and Nepal! The famous Jaldapara has one of the highest elephant densities in India. Tea gardens often face elephant intrusions.

notable_wildlife.name: Indian One-horned Rhinoceros
notable_wildlife.funfact: West Bengal has India's second-largest rhino population after Assam! Jaldapara has over 300 rhinos. Their 'armor-plated' skin is actually quite soft. They can run at 55 km/h despite weighing 2,000+ kg!

notable_wildlife.name: Saltwater Crocodile
notable_wildlife.funfact: The world's largest living reptile! Sundarbans crocodiles can grow over 6 meters. They can swim 1,000 km in open ocean. They coexist with tigers - both are apex predators in the mangroves!

notable_wildlife.name: Gangetic Dolphin
notable_wildlife.funfact: These dolphins are effectively blind! They navigate murky rivers using echolocation. They swim on their side. The Hooghly River near Kolkata still has a small population despite heavy shipping traffic.

notable_wildlife.name: Clouded Leopard
notable_wildlife.funfact: The smallest of the big cats! They have the longest canine teeth relative to body size. They can climb down trees headfirst. Buxa Tiger Reserve is one of the best places to spot them in India.

sundarbans_ecosystem.name: Sundarbans
sundarbans_ecosystem.key_species: Royal Bengal Tiger, Saltwater Crocodile, Gangetic Dolphin, Fishing Cat
sundarbans_ecosystem.funfact: The ONLY mangrove forest where tigers live! Tigers here swim between islands hunting deer, wild boar, and even fishing. They've killed more humans than any other tiger population. Locals wear masks on the back of their heads because tigers prefer attacking from behind!

national_parks.name: Sundarbans National Park
national_parks.key_species: Tiger, Saltwater Crocodile, Fishing Cat, Irrawaddy Dolphin
national_parks.funfact: The core area of the Sundarbans! Tigers here have become semi-aquatic. The forest is named after 'Sundari' trees (Heritiera fomes). It's a labyrinth of over 100 islands. Tides rise up to 5 meters daily!

national_parks.name: Jaldapara National Park
national_parks.key_species: Indian Rhino, Elephant, Tiger, Gaur
national_parks.funfact: Has India's second-largest rhino population! The tall elephant grass provides perfect cover for rhinos. Elephant safari is the best way to see wildlife. Was upgraded from wildlife sanctuary to national park in 2012.

national_parks.name: Gorumara National Park
national_parks.key_species: Indian Rhino, Elephant, Gaur, Wild Boar
national_parks.funfact: One of the best places to see Indian rhinos up close! The Jaldhaka River flows through. Has excellent watchtowers for wildlife viewing. The grasslands are burned annually to promote new growth for herbivores.

national_parks.name: Buxa Tiger Reserve
national_parks.key_species: Tiger, Elephant, Clouded Leopard, Gaur
national_parks.funfact: Borders Bhutan and has exceptional biodiversity! Contains the historic Buxa Fort used as a detention camp during India's freedom struggle. The Jayanti River creates beautiful forests. Has 230+ bird species.

migratory_birds.funfact: Asia's second-largest bird colony! Over 100,000 birds from Siberia winter here. The trees turn white with bird droppings! The storks arrive in June and leave by December.

ut_animal.name: Dugong (Sea Cow)
ut_animal.funfact: Dugongs are the only herbivorous marine mammals! They 'mow' seagrass like underwater cows. They were likely the inspiration for mermaid legends. A & N Islands have India's largest dugong population - about 200 individuals.

ut_bird.name: Andaman Wood Pigeon
ut_bird.funfact: Found ONLY in the Andaman Islands! They're larger than common pigeons with metallic green plumage. They feed in the forest canopy and rarely come to the ground. Their booming calls echo through the forests.

ut_tree.name: Andaman Padauk
ut_tree.funfact: One of the most valuable timbers in the world! The wood has beautiful red-orange color. It was overexploited but now protected. A single Padauk tree can be worth lakhs of rupees!

notable_wildlife.name: Saltwater Crocodile
notable_wildlife.funfact: The world's largest reptile! Andaman 'salties' grow over 6 meters and can weigh 1,000+ kg. They swim between islands and have been seen 100 km from shore. They're responsible for occasional human fatalities.

notable_wildlife.name: Andaman Wild Pig
notable_wildlife.funfact: An endemic subspecies found nowhere else! They're smaller than mainland wild pigs. They're a crucial food source for indigenous tribes. Feral dogs are their main threat.

notable_wildlife.name: Narcondam Hornbill
notable_wildlife.funfact: Found ONLY on one tiny volcanic island (7 sq km)! The entire world population of about 400 birds lives on this single island. If the island's forest is destroyed, the species goes extinct.

notable_wildlife.name: Andaman Day Gecko
notable_wildlife.funfact: One of the few gecko species active during daytime! They have brilliant green coloration. Unlike most geckos, they lack sticky toe pads and rely on claws for climbing.

notable_wildlife.name: Leatherback Sea Turtle
notable_wildlife.funfact: The world's largest turtle - up to 3 meters long and 900 kg! They dive deeper than any other turtle (1,200 meters). They can maintain body temperature in cold water. India's best nesting beaches are here.

notable_wildlife.name: Coconut Crab
notable_wildlife.funfact: The world's largest land invertebrate - leg span up to 1 meter! They can crack coconuts with their powerful claws. They climb trees and drop coconuts to eat. They can lift 28 kg - heavier than a small child!

endemic_species.name: Andaman Serpent Eagle
endemic_species.funfact: Endemic to Andaman Islands! Specialized in hunting snakes. They can spot a snake from 100 meters away. Their short crest distinguishes them from mainland serpent eagles.

endemic_species.name: Nicobar Megapode
endemic_species.funfact: These birds don't incubate their eggs - they bury them in mounds of decaying vegetation! The heat from decomposition incubates the eggs. Chicks dig themselves out and are independent from day one.

endemic_species.name: Andaman Cobra
endemic_species.funfact: Found only in Andaman Islands! They're smaller than mainland cobras. Unlike other cobras, they rarely raise a hood when threatened. They're the top snake predator on the islands.

coral_reef_ecosystem.funfact: Andaman has India's best coral reefs! The 2004 tsunami actually exposed new corals. Some reefs here are 8,000 years old. Mahatma Gandhi Marine National Park has spectacular underwater biodiversity.

national_parks.name: Mahatma Gandhi Marine National Park
national_parks.key_species: Coral reefs, Sea turtles, Dolphins, 300+ fish species
national_parks.funfact: India's only marine national park where you can walk on a coral reef at low tide! Glass-bottom boat rides show underwater wonders. The park has 15 islands with pristine beaches.

national_parks.name: Campbell Bay National Park
national_parks.key_species: Saltwater Crocodile, Nicobar Megapode, Coconut Crab
national_parks.funfact: India's southernmost national park! Closer to Indonesia than to mainland India. The indigenous Shompen tribe lives in the buffer zone. Mount Thullier (642m) is the highest peak.

ut_bird.name: Indian Grey Hornbill
ut_bird.funfact: Despite being a city, Chandigarh has thriving hornbill populations! They nest in tree holes in Sector 17 and Rock Garden. They eat figs and regurgitate seeds, planting trees across the city.

notable_wildlife.name: Nilgai
notable_wildlife.funfact: Asia's largest antelope lives on the outskirts of India's most planned city! They often wander into residential areas. Despite weighing 300 kg, they can jump 1.5-meter fences!

notable_wildlife.name: Sambar Deer
notable_wildlife.funfact: India's largest deer! They come to Sukhna Lake to drink at dawn and dusk. Their alarm call warns all forest creatures of predators. Males have magnificent antlers that shed annually.

notable_wildlife.name: Indian Leopard
notable_wildlife.funfact: Leopards occasionally venture into Chandigarh from surrounding forests! They've been spotted in sectors near the hills. The city's green cover and deer population attract them.

sukhna_lake_ecosystem.funfact: Created by Le Corbusier in 1958! Now a Ramsar Wetland of International Importance. Thousands of migratory birds from Siberia winter here. The lake is motor-boat free to protect wildlife.

notable_wildlife.name: Leopard
notable_wildlife.location: Forested areas of Dadra Nagar Haveli
notable_wildlife.funfact: Despite being a small UT, leopards still survive in the forested hills! They prey on wild pigs and deer. Camera traps have captured them near village edges.

notable_wildlife.name: Indian Flying Fox
notable_wildlife.funfact: India's largest bat with a wingspan of 1.5 meters! They roost in trees in huge colonies of 5,000+ bats. They fly up to 50 km each night to feed on fruit. They're crucial pollinators for many trees.

marine_life.funfact: Diu's waters host migrating whale sharks in winter! Local fishermen now protect them instead of hunting. The beaches are nesting sites for Olive Ridley turtles.

ut_bird.name: House Sparrow
ut_bird.funfact: Once abundant, Delhi's sparrows have declined by 80%! Causes include radiation, fewer mud houses, and pesticides. Conservation efforts include installing nest boxes. World Sparrow Day (March 20) raises awareness.

notable_wildlife.name: Nilgai
notable_wildlife.funfact: Asia's largest antelope lives in India's capital! They're seen regularly in the Ridge area and even near Parliament. They cause traffic jams when crossing roads!

notable_wildlife.name: Indian Peafowl
notable_wildlife.funfact: India's national bird thrives in Delhi! They roam gardens, university campuses, and even government buildings. Their calls are common in South Delhi neighborhoods.

notable_wildlife.name: Golden Jackal
notable_wildlife.funfact: Jackals survive in the middle of Delhi! They're nocturnal and rarely seen but their howling can be heard at dusk. They help control rodent populations. Camera traps have captured them near Rashtrapati Bhavan!

notable_wildlife.name: Painted Stork
notable_wildlife.funfact: Delhi Zoo has one of India's largest painted stork colonies! Over 2,000 birds nest here. They fly 50+ km daily to feed in wetlands around Delhi.

delhi_ridge.name: Delhi Ridge Forest
delhi_ridge.funfact: The Ridge is Delhi's 'green lung'! It's an extension of the Aravalli Hills - the world's oldest mountain range. Despite urban pressure, it hosts 450+ bird species. Ancient Mughal hunting lodges still exist within the forest.

okhla_bird_sanctuary.name: Okhla Bird Sanctuary
okhla_bird_sanctuary.key_species: 320+ bird species, including rare migratory birds
okhla_bird_sanctuary.funfact: A tiny wetland hosting 320+ bird species right on the Delhi-UP border! Siberian birds winter here. The Okhla Barrage creates ideal habitat. You can see 100+ species in a single day during winter!

Goa.already_covered: Covered in main states section above

ut_animal.name: Kashmir Stag (Hangul)
ut_animal.funfact: Found ONLY in Kashmir! Once numbering 5,000+, now fewer than 250 remain. It's the only surviving Asian subspecies of red deer. Males have magnificent antlers with 11-16 points. They shed and regrow antlers annually.

ut_bird.name: Black-necked Crane
ut_bird.funfact: The world's only alpine crane! They breed in Ladakh above 4,500m. They're sacred to Tibetan Buddhists who call them 'Heavenly Birds'. They mate for life and perform elaborate dances.

ut_tree.name: Chinar
ut_tree.funfact: The Chinar is Kashmir's iconic tree! Some trees are over 700 years old. The Chinar Bagh in Srinagar has trees planted by Mughal emperors. Their leaves turn spectacular red-gold in autumn.

notable_wildlife.name: Snow Leopard
notable_wildlife.funfact: J&K has significant snow leopard populations! They can jump 15 meters in one leap. Their tail is so thick they wrap it around their face while sleeping. They can take down prey three times their weight.

notable_wildlife.name: Himalayan Brown Bear
notable_wildlife.funfact: One of the rarest bear subspecies! Only about 500-750 remain. They're the apex predator above the treeline. Before hibernating, they can eat for 20 hours a day and gain 100 kg!

notable_wildlife.name: Markhor
notable_wildlife.funfact: Pakistan's national animal lives in J&K too! Males have spectacular spiraling horns up to 1.5 meters. They can climb near-vertical cliffs. Their name means 'snake eater' though they're herbivores.

notable_wildlife.name: Asiatic Black Bear
notable_wildlife.funfact: They have a distinctive white 'V' on their chest! They're excellent tree climbers. Man-bear conflicts are increasing as forests shrink. They can run 40 km/h despite their bulk.

notable_wildlife.name: Musk Deer
notable_wildlife.funfact: Males have vampire-like fangs instead of antlers! They produce musk worth $45,000/kg. They're heavily poached. One male has enough musk to make 50,000 perfume bottles!

national_parks.name: Dachigam National Park
national_parks.key_species: Hangul, Himalayan Black Bear, Leopard, Musk Deer
national_parks.funfact: The ONLY home of the critically endangered Hangul! 'Dachigam' means 'ten villages' - ten villages were relocated to create this park. The alpine meadows in summer are carpeted with wildflowers.

national_parks.name: Hemis National Park
national_parks.key_species: Snow Leopard, Bharal, Tibetan Wolf, Eurasian Brown Bear
national_parks.funfact: India's largest national park! Has the highest density of snow leopards in the world. Located in Ladakh's cold desert. Contains the famous Hemis Monastery. Completely road-less - only accessible by foot or horse.

national_parks.name: Kishtwar High Altitude National Park
national_parks.key_species: Himalayan Brown Bear, Snow Leopard, Serow
national_parks.funfact: One of the least explored parks in India! Has glaciers, alpine meadows, and dense forests. The Himalayan brown bear is the main attraction. Completely uninhabited and rarely visited.

dal_lake_ecosystem.name: Dal Lake
dal_lake_ecosystem.funfact: Called 'Jewel in the crown of Kashmir'! Supports unique floating gardens called 'Rad'. Over 150 bird species visit seasonally. The lake is a complex ecosystem with lotus roots, fish, and waterfowl coexisting.

ut_animal.name: Snow Leopard
ut_animal.funfact: Ladakh has the world's highest density of snow leopards! They're called 'Grey Ghosts' because they're so elusive. They can leap 15 meters in one bound. Their spots help them vanish against rocky slopes.

ut_bird.name: Black-necked Crane
ut_bird.funfact: The only crane that breeds at high altitude! Ladakh's Hanle and Tso Kar are crucial breeding sites. They fly to Tibet and back. Locals call them 'Cha Thungthung' and consider them sacred.

notable_wildlife.name: Wild Yak
notable_wildlife.funfact: Wild yaks can survive at 6,000m where oxygen is 40% lower! Their lungs and heart are three times larger than cattle. They have three times more red blood cells. They can smell predators from 2 km away!

notable_wildlife.name: Tibetan Wild Ass (Kiang)
notable_wildlife.funfact: The largest wild ass species! They can run at 70 km/h at altitudes where most animals can barely breathe. Herds of 100+ graze on the high plateau. They're more closely related to horses than donkeys.

notable_wildlife.name: Tibetan Wolf
notable_wildlife.funfact: One of the least-studied wolves! They're larger than Indian wolves with thicker fur. They prey on bharal, marmots, and even yaks. They've learned to hunt in Ladakh's extreme cold where other predators can't survive.

notable_wildlife.name: Bharal (Blue Sheep)
notable_wildlife.funfact: Neither sheep nor goat - they're an evolutionary link! Their blue-gray coat provides perfect camouflage. They can navigate near-vertical cliffs. Snow leopards depend on them as primary prey.

notable_wildlife.name: Pallas's Cat (Manul)
notable_wildlife.funfact: The world's grumpiest-looking cat! Their flat face and round pupils make them look perpetually angry. They have the longest and densest fur of any cat. They can survive -50°C temperatures.

notable_wildlife.name: Himalayan Marmot
notable_wildlife.funfact: These giant rodents hibernate for 8 months! They whistle to warn of predators. They live in family groups in extensive burrow systems. They're the main prey of golden eagles in Ladakh.

high_altitude_lakes.key_species: Bar-headed Goose, Black-necked Crane, Brahminy Duck
high_altitude_lakes.funfact: Pangong Tso is one of the world's highest saltwater lakes at 4,350m! It changes color from blue to green to red. Tso Moriri has breeding bar-headed geese that fly over Everest during migration!

national_parks.name: Hemis National Park
national_parks.key_species: Snow Leopard, Bharal, Wild Yak, Tibetan Wolf
national_parks.funfact: India's largest national park and world's best place to see snow leopards! Has around 200 snow leopards - the highest density anywhere. The entire park is above 3,000m altitude. Completely uninhabited.

national_parks.name: Karakoram Wildlife Sanctuary
national_parks.key_species: Snow Leopard, Tibetan Wolf, Wild Yak
national_parks.funfact: One of the highest wildlife sanctuaries in the world! Contains some of the world's largest glaciers. Extremely remote and rarely visited. Has the rare Ladakh urial sheep.

marine_ecosystem.funfact: India's smallest UT and only coral atoll system! The islands are tiny - total land area is just 32 sq km. The lagoons are so clear you can see 50 meters deep. Some atolls are only 2 meters above sea level!

notable_wildlife.name: Hawksbill Sea Turtle
notable_wildlife.funfact: Critically endangered turtles nest here! Their beak-like mouth is perfect for eating sponges in coral crevices. The beautiful shell made them targets for poachers. Now strictly protected.

notable_wildlife.name: Green Sea Turtle
notable_wildlife.funfact: Green turtles get their name from green fat (not shell)! They're the only herbivorous sea turtle, grazing on seagrass. They return to the same beach where they hatched, navigating thousands of kilometers.

notable_wildlife.name: Manta Ray
notable_wildlife.funfact: The world's largest ray with a 7-meter wingspan! They have the largest brain of any fish and can recognize themselves in mirrors. Lakshadweep is one of the best places in India to see them.

notable_wildlife.name: Whale Shark
notable_wildlife.funfact: The world's largest fish visits Lakshadweep waters! Despite their size (up to 12m), they eat only plankton and small fish. They're gentle giants and completely harmless to humans.

notable_wildlife.name: Spinner Dolphin
notable_wildlife.funfact: Named for their acrobatic spinning jumps! They can spin up to 7 times in one leap. Pods of 1,000+ dolphins are common around Lakshadweep. They're incredibly social and playful.

coral_biodiversity.funfact: Lakshadweep's coral reefs are among the healthiest in India! The lagoons host giant clams, parrotfish, and reef sharks. Climate change and coral bleaching are major threats. The atolls exist only because of coral growth.

endemic_species.name: Lakshadweep Sea Slug
endemic_species.funfact: Several sea slug species are found only in Lakshadweep! These colorful creatures are essentially snails without shells. Some species can photosynthesize like plants using algae they eat!

notable_wildlife.name: Olive Ridley Sea Turtle
notable_wildlife.funfact: Puducherry's beaches are turtle nesting sites! The French-era laws actually protected turtles. Local fishermen now help protect nests. Hatchlings instinctively crawl toward the sea at night.

notable_wildlife.name: Bottlenose Dolphin
notable_wildlife.funfact: Dolphins are regularly seen from Puducherry's beaches! They're incredibly intelligent - can recognize themselves in mirrors. They sleep with one eye open! They've been known to rescue drowning humans.

ousteri_lake.name: Ousteri Lake (Oussudu Lake)
ousteri_lake.funfact: One of the most important wetlands in South India! Over 40,000 migratory birds visit annually. Spot-billed Pelicans breed here. Despite being near Puducherry city, it remains a crucial bird habitat.

summary_statistics.funfact: India has only 2.4% of the world's land area but hosts 7-8% of all recorded species! It's one of 17 'megadiverse' countries. The Western Ghats alone have more endemic species than most countries!
